1	\chapter{Effective descent: restriction of the glued sheaf to a chart}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GlueDescent}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The gluing primitive \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} produces, out of a glue datum
7	\(D\) of schemes (index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap
8	immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), transition isomorphisms
9	\(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), glued scheme \(X\) with chart
10	immersions \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\)), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
11	\(\mathcal{M}\) as the descent equalizer
12	\[
13	  \mathcal{M} \;=\; \operatorname{eq}(a, b)
14	    \;\hookrightarrow\; \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
15	\]
16	of two parallel maps into the overlap product. That construction makes
17	\(\mathcal{M}\) but does \emph{not} yet consume the cocycle conditions (C1)/(C2) on
18	the transition family \((g_{ij})\). This chapter discharges the remaining content of
19	descent: it shows that restricting the glued sheaf back to a chart recovers the
20	input sheaf there. Concretely, the canonical \emph{restriction morphism}
21	\(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{M}_i\) -- the adjoint transpose along the chart
22	immersion \(\iota_i\) of the \(i\)-th equalizer projection -- is an isomorphism, and
23	it is here that (C1)/(C2) are used. The engine is that pullback along an open
24	immersion is, up to natural isomorphism, the site-level restriction functor
25	(\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}); being a right adjoint it
26	preserves the descent equalizer and the pushforward product, so the chart
27	restriction of \(\mathcal{M}\) is computed factor by factor. The non-formal input is
28	the \emph{overlap base change} \(\beta_{ij}\), which identifies the restricted
29	pushforward factor \(\iota_i^{*}(\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\) with a pushforward of a
30	restriction of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) to the overlap, using the cartesianness of the
31	chart--overlap square.
32	
33	\section{The descent equalizer and its overlap base change}
34	\label{sec:gd_equalizer}
35	
36	\begin{definition}
37	
38	\leanok
39	[The pushforward product carrying the descent equalizer]
40	  \label{def:gr_glue_equalizer}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueProd}
42	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue}
43	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum and \((\mathcal{M}_i)_{i \in J}\) a family of sheaves of
44	  modules with \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\). Write
45	  \[
46	    P \;=\; \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
47	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
48	    Q \;=\; \prod_{(i,j)} (f_{ij} \circ \iota_i)_{*}\,\bigl(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\bigr)
49	  \]
50	  for the \emph{pushforward product} and the \emph{overlap product} of sheaves of
51	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The two descent legs of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue},
52	  \[
53	    P \;\underset{b}{\overset{a}{\rightrightarrows}}\; Q,
54	  \]
55	  are: on the \((i,j)\)-component, leg \(a\) restricts the \(i\)-th chart factor to
56	  the overlap \(V_{ij}\) using the unit of the pullback--pushforward adjunction along
57	  \(f_{ij}\) and the pushforward-composition comparison
58	  \((\iota_i)_{*} \cong (f_{ij} \circ \iota_i)_{*} \circ f_{ij}^{*}\); leg \(b\)
59	  restricts the \(j\)-th chart factor, transports it across the inverse transition
60	  isomorphism \(g_{ij}^{-1}\), and reindexes the immersion via the glue condition
61	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji}) \circ \iota_j = f_{ij} \circ \iota_i\). The glued sheaf
62	  \(\mathcal{M} = \operatorname{eq}(a,b)\) is the equalizer of this parallel pair;
63	  \(P\) is the object it embeds into and \(Q\) the object detecting the overlap
64	  agreement. The product \(P\) is the carrier on which the \(i\)-th projection (and,
65	  through it, the restriction morphism) is defined.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}
69	
70	\leanok
71	[Overlap base change of the chart square (\(\beta_{ij}\))]
72	  \label{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}
74	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
75	  For chart indices \(i, j \in J\) there is a natural isomorphism of functors
76	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_{U_j}) \to \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_{U_i})\),
77	  \[
78	    \beta_{ij} \;:\;
79	      \iota_i^{*}\,(\iota_j)_{*}
80	        \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
81	      (f_{ij})_{*}\,(t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*},
82	  \]
83	  identifying, for any sheaf \(\mathcal{N}\) on the chart \(U_j\), the restriction to
84	  the chart \(U_i\) of the pushforward \((\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{N}\) with the
85	  pushforward along \(f_{ij}\) of the restriction of \(\mathcal{N}\) to the overlap
86	  \(V_{ij}\) along \(t_{ij} \circ f_{ji}\). Here the chart restriction \(\iota_i^{*}\)
87	  and the overlap restriction \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\) are realised as the
88	  site-level restriction functors, isomorphic to the geometric pullbacks by
89	  \cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}.
90	\end{lemma}
91	\begin{proof}
92	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:gr_overlap_appIso_compat,
93	    lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_inv_app, lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_hom_app}
94	  The isomorphism is the cartesianness of the chart--overlap square, made site-level.
95	  The square
96	  \[
97	    \begin{array}{ccc}
98	      V_{ij} & \xrightarrow{\ t_{ij} \circ f_{ji}\ } & U_j \\
99	      \ \downarrow{\scriptstyle f_{ij}} & & \downarrow{\scriptstyle \iota_j} \\
100	      U_i & \xrightarrow{\ \ \iota_i\ \ } & X
101	    \end{array}
102	  \]
103	  is cartesian, and its cartesianness is visible already on underlying opens: for an
104	  open \(V \subseteq U_i\),
105	  \[
106	    \iota_j^{-1}\bigl(\iota_i(V)\bigr)
107	      \;=\; (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})\bigl(f_{ij}^{-1}(V)\bigr),
108	  \]
109	  a point of \(U_j\) lands in the image of \(V\) under \(\iota_i\) precisely when it
110	  comes, via the glue relation, from a point of the overlap lying over \(V\). Hence
111	  the two composite opens functors
112	  \[
113	    (\iota_i)_{!} \circ \iota_j^{-1}
114	      \;=\;
115	    f_{ij}^{-1} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})_{!}
116	  \]
117	  agree on objects, and -- morphisms of the opens poset being proof-irrelevant -- as
118	  functors. Both \(\iota_i^{*}(\iota_j)_{*}\) and \((f_{ij})_{*}(t_{ij}\circ
119	  f_{ji})^{*}\) are site-level pushforwards along these two (equal) opens functors, so
120	  the comparison is assembled from the pushforward-composition isomorphisms together
121	  with the natural isomorphism transporting one opens functor to the other:
122	  \[
123	    \beta_{ij}
124	      \;=\;
125	    (\text{pushforwardComp})
126	      \;\circ\;
127	    (\text{pushforward of the opens-functor equality})
128	      \;\circ\;
129	    (\text{pushforwardCongr})
130	      \;\circ\;
131	    (\text{pushforwardComp})^{-1}.
132	  \]
133	  The one substantive coherence to check is that the comparison glued from the
134	  object-level opens equality is natural in the open \(V\): on the section level the
135	  two restriction maps induced by the equal composite functors coincide because the
136	  connecting morphisms are the unique arrows of the opens poset between the identified
137	  opens. This is the pointwise compatibility that completes the \(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\)
138	  factor.
139	\end{proof}
140	
141	\section{The restriction morphism and effective descent}
142	\label{sec:gd_descent}
143	
144	\begin{lemma}
145	
146	\leanok
147	[The chart restriction morphism of the glued sheaf]
148	  \label{lem:glueRestrictionHom}
149	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionHom}
150	  \uses{def:gr_glue_equalizer, def:scheme_modules_glue}
151	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be the glued sheaf of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. The
152	  \(i\)-th \emph{projection} of \(\mathcal{M}\),
153	  \[
154	    \pi_i \;:\; \mathcal{M} \longrightarrow (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i,
155	  \]
156	  is the equalizer inclusion \(\mathcal{M} \hookrightarrow P\)
157	  (\cref{def:gr_glue_equalizer}) followed by the \(i\)-th product projection. Its
158	  adjoint transpose along the chart immersion \(\iota_i\), under the
159	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \(\iota_i^{*} \dashv (\iota_i)_{*}\), is the
160	  \emph{restriction morphism}
161	  \[
162	    \mathrm{r}_i \;:\; \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}_i .
163	  \]
164	  Equivalently, \(\mathrm{r}_i\) is the unique morphism whose pushforward, precomposed
165	  with the unit, recovers \(\pi_i\).
166	\end{lemma}
167	\begin{proof}
168	  \uses{def:gr_glue_equalizer, def:scheme_modules_glue}
169	  The projection \(\pi_i\) is well-defined by \cref{def:gr_glue_equalizer}: the glued
170	  sheaf is the equalizer \(\operatorname{eq}(a,b) \hookrightarrow P\), and composing
171	  the inclusion with the \(i\)-th projection of the product \(P = \prod_i
172	  (\iota_i)_{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) gives a map to \((\iota_i)_{*}\mathcal{M}_i\). The
173	  restriction morphism is then its image under the natural bijection
174	  \[
175	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(\mathcal{M}, (\iota_i)_{*}\mathcal{M}_i\bigr)
176	      \;\cong\;
177	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_{U_i}}\bigl(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{M}_i\bigr)
178	  \]
179	  supplied by the adjunction \(\iota_i^{*} \dashv (\iota_i)_{*}\). The stated
180	  equivalent description is the unit--counit form of this transpose.
181	\end{proof}
182	
183	\begin{theorem}
184	
185	\leanok
186	[Effective descent: the chart restriction morphism is an isomorphism]
187	  \label{thm:isIso_glueRestrictionHom}
188	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_glueRestrictionHom}
189	  \uses{lem:glueRestrictionHom, lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso,
190	    lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib, lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib,
191	    def:gr_glueRestrictionInv, lem:gr_glueRestrict_hom_ext,
192	    lem:gr_glueChartComponent_self_counit,
193	    lem:gr_glueRestrictionHom_glueChartComponent,
194	    lem:gr_glueRestrictionInv_proj}
195	  Assume the transition family \((g_{ij})\) satisfies the cocycle conditions (C1) and
196	  (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. Then for every chart index \(i\) the
197	  restriction morphism \(\mathrm{r}_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{M}_i\)
198	  (\cref{lem:glueRestrictionHom}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of
199	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules.
200	\end{theorem}
201	\begin{proof}
202	  \uses{lem:glueRestrictionHom, lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso,
203	    lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib, lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib,
204	    def:gr_glueRestrictionInv, lem:gr_glueRestrict_hom_ext,
205	    lem:gr_glueChartComponent_self_counit,
206	    lem:gr_glueRestrictionHom_glueChartComponent,
207	    lem:gr_glueRestrictionInv_proj}
208	  The chart pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) is naturally isomorphic to the site-level
209	  restriction functor (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}), which
210	  is a right adjoint; consequently \(\iota_i^{*}\) \emph{preserves limits}. Applying
211	  it to the descent equalizer \(\mathcal{M} = \operatorname{eq}(a,b)\) exhibits
212	  \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) as the equalizer of the restricted legs
213	  \(\iota_i^{*}a, \iota_i^{*}b\), and applying it to the product
214	  \(P = \prod_j (\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\) exhibits \(\iota_i^{*}P\) as the product
215	  \(\prod_j \iota_i^{*}(\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\). The overlap base change
216	  \(\beta_{ij}\) of \cref{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso} then identifies the \(j\)-th
217	  factor \(\iota_i^{*}(\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\) with
218	  \((f_{ij})_{*}\bigl((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\bigr)\). Under these
219	  identifications the restricted descent data becomes the data attached to the chart
220	  \(U_i\) by the cover \(\{f_{ij}\}\) of \(U_i\), and the \(i\)-th factor (where
221	  \(j = i\)) is, by (C1), \(\mathcal{M}_i\) itself.
222	
223	  We produce an inverse \(\mathrm{s}_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\).
224	  Because \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is the equalizer of the restricted legs, it
225	  suffices to give a morphism \(\mathcal{M}_i \to \iota_i^{*}P\) -- equivalently a
226	  compatible family of morphisms into the factors -- that equalizes
227	  \(\iota_i^{*}a\) and \(\iota_i^{*}b\). The \(j\)-th component is
228	  \[
229	    \mathcal{M}_i
230	      \xrightarrow{\ \eta_{f_{ij}}\ }
231	    (f_{ij})_{*}\,f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i
232	      \xrightarrow{\ (f_{ij})_{*} g_{ij}\ }
233	    (f_{ij})_{*}\,(t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j
234	      \xrightarrow{\ \beta_{ij}^{-1}\ }
235	    \iota_i^{*}(\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j ,
236	  \]
237	  the unit of the pullback--pushforward adjunction along \(f_{ij}\), followed by the
238	  pushforward of the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\), followed by the inverse of the
239	  overlap base change. That this family equalizes the two restricted legs is exactly
240	  the triple-overlap multiplicativity (C2), transported through \(\beta\); and the
241	  composite \(\mathrm{r}_i \circ \mathrm{s}_i = \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{M}_i}\) reduces,
242	  via the \(j = i\) component, to the self-identity (C1) together with the counit
243	  triangle identity of the adjunction. The reverse composite
244	  \(\mathrm{s}_i \circ \mathrm{r}_i = \mathrm{id}\) holds because both sides are
245	  equalizer maps with the same components into \(P\); two such agree once their
246	  factors agree, which is the separatedness of the sheaf equalizer over the chart
247	  cover. The limit computation underlying these equalities is the sheaf
248	  Mayer--Vietoris pullback square (\cref{lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib}), and the
249	  final identification of sections is the uniqueness-over-a-cover principle
250	  (\cref{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}). Hence \(\mathrm{r}_i\) is an isomorphism.
251	\end{proof}
252	
253	\section{Construction of the inverse: the descent obligations}
254	\label{sec:gd_inverse}
255	
256	This section records, as sublemmas of \cref{thm:isIso_glueRestrictionHom}, the
257	internal machinery of the proof: the structure-sheaf compatibilities feeding the
258	overlap base change \(\beta_{ij}\), the bridges between the geometric and the
259	site-level pullback--pushforward adjunctions, the explicit construction of the
260	candidate inverse \(\mathrm{s}_i\) as an equalizer lift, and the three obligations
261	that consume the cocycle conditions (C1)/(C2). Every block carries a one-to-one Lean
262	declaration.
263	
264	\subsection{Section-level compatibilities of the overlap square}
265	
266	\begin{lemma}[Transport of \(\mathrm{appLE}\) along equal morphisms]
267	  \label{lem:gr_appLE_congr_mor}
268	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.appLE_congr_mor}
269	  Let \(f, g : A \to B\) be morphisms of schemes with \(f = g\), let \(U\) be an open of
270	  \(B\) and \(W\) an open of \(A\) with \(W \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) (equivalently
271	  \(W \subseteq g^{-1}(U)\)). Then the two induced section maps
272	  \(\Gamma(B, U) \to \Gamma(A, W)\) along \(f\) and along \(g\) coincide:
273	  \(f^{\mathrm{LE}}_{U,W} = g^{\mathrm{LE}}_{U,W}\).
274	\end{lemma}
275	\begin{proof}
276	  Substituting the equality \(f = g\) reduces both sides to the same map; the two
277	  open-inclusion witnesses \(W \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) and \(W \subseteq g^{-1}(U)\) are
278	  proof-irrelevant.
279	\end{proof}
280	
281	\begin{lemma}[Structure-sheaf compatibility of the chart--overlap square]
282	  \label{lem:gr_overlap_appIso_compat}
283	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_overlap_appIso_compat}
284	  \uses{lem:gr_appLE_congr_mor, def:scheme_modules_glue}
285	  For chart indices \(i, j\) and an open \(V \subseteq U_i\), the two composite
286	  section maps
287	  \[
288	    \Gamma(U_i, V)\;\longrightarrow\;
289	      \Gamma\bigl(U_j,\ (t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})\bigl(f_{ij}^{-1}(V)\bigr)\bigr)
290	  \]
291	  of the chart--overlap square agree. The first goes ``through the glued scheme'':
292	  the inverse of the comparison isomorphism of \(\iota_i\) on \(V\), the section map of
293	  \(\iota_j\) over \(\iota_i(V)\), and the restriction along the opens identity
294	  \(\iota_j^{-1}(\iota_i(V)) = (t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})(f_{ij}^{-1}(V))\). The second goes
295	  ``through the overlap'': the section map of \(f_{ij}\) over \(V\) followed by the
296	  inverse comparison isomorphism of \(t_{ij}\circ f_{ji}\).
297	\end{lemma}
298	\begin{proof}
299	  \uses{lem:gr_appLE_congr_mor, def:scheme_modules_glue}
300	  Both composites are \(\mathrm{appLE}\)s of the two composite morphisms of the square,
301	  which are equal by the glue condition \((t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})\circ \iota_j =
302	  f_{ij}\circ \iota_i\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. Moving the two comparison
303	  isomorphisms to \(\mathrm{appLE}\) form (using \(\mathrm{appIso}\) as an
304	  \(\mathrm{appLE}\), the composition law \(g^{\mathrm{LE}}\circ f^{\mathrm{LE}} =
305	  (f\circ g)^{\mathrm{LE}}\), and the absorption of presheaf restrictions into the
306	  open-inclusion witness) reduces the claim to the equality of the two
307	  \(\mathrm{appLE}\)s of the equal composites, which is \cref{lem:gr_appLE_congr_mor}.
308	\end{proof}
309	
310	\begin{lemma}[Sections of the overlap base change, inverse side]
311	  \label{lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_inv_app}
312	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueOverlapBaseChangeIso_inv_app_app}
313	  \uses{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}
314	  For a sheaf \(\mathcal{N}\) on \(U_j\) and an open \(V \subseteq U_i\), the inverse
315	  of the overlap base change \(\beta_{ij}^{-1}\), evaluated on sections over \(V\), is
316	  the restriction map of \(\mathcal{N}\) along the opens identity
317	  \(\iota_j^{-1}(\iota_i(V)) = (t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})(f_{ij}^{-1}(V))\) of
318	  \cref{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}:
319	  \[
320	    (\beta_{ij}^{-1})_{\mathcal{N}}\big|_V
321	      \;=\;
322	    \mathcal{N}\!\left(\,(t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})(f_{ij}^{-1}V) \;=\; \iota_j^{-1}(\iota_i V)\,\right).
323	  \]
324	\end{lemma}
325	\begin{proof}
326	  \uses{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}
327	  Each of the four functorial factors of \(\beta_{ij}\) (\cref{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso})
328	  acts on sections over \(V\) either as the identity or as a single presheaf
329	  restriction along the opens identity; composing them leaves exactly the named
330	  restriction map. The verification is pointwise on sections.
331	\end{proof}
332	
333	\begin{lemma}[Sections of the overlap base change, forward side]
334	  \label{lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_hom_app}
335	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueOverlapBaseChangeIso_hom_app_app}
336	  \uses{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}
337	  Symmetrically, the forward overlap base change \(\beta_{ij}\), evaluated on sections
338	  over an open \(V \subseteq U_i\), is the restriction map of \(\mathcal{N}\) along the
339	  reverse opens identity \(\iota_j^{-1}(\iota_i(V)) = (t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})(f_{ij}^{-1}(V))\).
340	\end{lemma}
341	\begin{proof}
342	  \uses{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}
343	  Identical to \cref{lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_inv_app} with the roles of source and
344	  target opens exchanged: pointwise on sections, the composite of the four factors is
345	  the single presheaf restriction along the (reversed) opens identity.
346	\end{proof}
347	
348	\subsection{Bridges between the geometric and the site-level adjunction}
349	
350	The chart pullback \(\iota^{*}\) is built by sheafification and carries the
351	\emph{geometric} adjunction \(\iota^{*} \dashv \iota_{*}\); along an open immersion the
352	\emph{site-level} restriction functor \(\iota^{\mathrm{res}}\) carries a second
353	adjunction with concrete unit and counit. They share the right adjoint \(\iota_{*}\),
354	and \cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib} is the uniqueness comparison
355	of left adjoints between them. The next blocks transport units, counits and
356	congruence casts across that comparison, so that the descent obligations can be
357	discharged by section-level computations.
358	
359	\begin{lemma}[Unit comparison across the adjunctions]
360	  \label{lem:gr_restrictAdjunction_unit_iso}
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictAdjunction_unit_app_iso}
362	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
363	  For an open immersion \(f\) and a sheaf \(\mathcal{N}\) on the target, the geometric
364	  unit is the site-level unit followed by the pushforward of the left-adjoint
365	  comparison isomorphism:
366	  \[
367	    \eta^{\mathrm{res}}_{\mathcal{N}}\ \circ\
368	      f_{*}\bigl((\text{comparison})_{\mathcal{N}}\bigr)
369	    \;=\; \eta^{\mathrm{geo}}_{\mathcal{N}}.
370	  \]
371	\end{lemma}
372	\begin{proof}
373	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
374	  This is the general identity relating the unit of an adjunction to the unit of any
375	  other adjunction with the same right adjoint, transported by the uniqueness
376	  isomorphism of left adjoints (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}).
377	\end{proof}
378	
379	\begin{lemma}[Counit comparison across the adjunctions]
380	  \label{lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_counit}
381	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback_hom_app_counit}
382	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
383	  Dually, the left-adjoint comparison isomorphism on \(f_{*}\mathcal{N}\) followed by
384	  the geometric counit equals the site-level counit:
385	  \[
386	    (\text{comparison})_{f_{*}\mathcal{N}}\ \circ\ \varepsilon^{\mathrm{geo}}_{\mathcal{N}}
387	      \;=\; \varepsilon^{\mathrm{res}}_{\mathcal{N}}.
388	  \]
389	\end{lemma}
390	\begin{proof}
391	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
392	  The dual of \cref{lem:gr_restrictAdjunction_unit_iso}: the counit of one adjunction
393	  is conjugated to the counit of the other by the uniqueness comparison of left
394	  adjoints.
395	\end{proof}
396	
397	\begin{lemma}[Congruence cast at the pullback level]
398	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom}
399	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr_hom_app_eqToHom}
400	  For equal morphisms \(\varphi = \psi : X \to Y\) and a sheaf \(\mathcal{N}\) on \(Y\),
401	  the congruence isomorphism \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{N} \cong \psi^{*}\mathcal{N}\)
402	  induced by \(\varphi = \psi\) is the canonical transport (the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) of
403	  the object-level equality \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{N} = \psi^{*}\mathcal{N}\)).
404	\end{lemma}
405	\begin{proof}
406	  Substituting \(\varphi = \psi\) reduces the congruence cast to the identity, which is
407	  the canonical transport of a reflexivity.
408	\end{proof}
409	
410	\begin{lemma}[Trivial site-level congruence cast]
411	  \label{lem:gr_restrictFunctorCongr_rfl}
412	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorCongr_rfl_hom_app}
413	  For an open immersion \(\varphi\) the site-level congruence cast associated to the
414	  reflexivity \(\varphi = \varphi\) is the identity natural transformation.
415	\end{lemma}
416	\begin{proof}
417	  Pointwise on sections the cast is the presheaf restriction along a self-equality of
418	  opens, which is the identity by functoriality.
419	\end{proof}
420	
421	\begin{lemma}[Comparison intertwines the two congruence casts]
422	  \label{lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_congr}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback_congr}
424	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom, lem:gr_restrictFunctorCongr_rfl,
425	    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
426	  For equal open immersions \(\varphi = \psi : X \to Y\) the left-adjoint comparison
427	  isomorphism intertwines the site-level and the pullback-level congruence casts:
428	  \[
429	    (\text{comparison}_\varphi)_{\mathcal{N}}\ \circ\
430	      (\varphi^{*}\!=\psi^{*})_{\mathcal{N}}
431	    \;=\;
432	    (\varphi^{\mathrm{res}}\!=\psi^{\mathrm{res}})_{\mathcal{N}}\ \circ\
433	      (\text{comparison}_\psi)_{\mathcal{N}}.
434	  \]
435	\end{lemma}
436	\begin{proof}
437	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom, lem:gr_restrictFunctorCongr_rfl}
438	  Substituting \(\varphi = \psi\) collapses both congruence casts to identities
439	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom}, \cref{lem:gr_restrictFunctorCongr_rfl}),
440	  and the two comparison isomorphisms then coincide.
441	\end{proof}
442	
443	\subsection{The candidate inverse}
444	
445	\begin{definition}[Overlap base change in pullback form]
446	  \label{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso}
447	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueOverlapFactorIso}
448	  \uses{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
449	  Conjugating the overlap base change \(\beta_{ij}\)
450	  (\cref{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}) on both sides by the comparison
451	  \cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib} between the site-level
452	  restriction and the geometric pullback yields the isomorphism, in geometric form,
453	  \[
454	    \gamma_{ij} \;:\;
455	      \iota_i^{*}\bigl((\iota_j)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j\bigr)
456	        \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
457	      (f_{ij})_{*}\bigl((t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j\bigr),
458	  \]
459	  evaluated at the input sheaf \(\mathcal{M}_j\).
460	\end{definition}
461	
462	\begin{definition}[The \(j\)-th component of the candidate inverse]
463	  \label{def:gr_glueChartComponent}
464	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueChartComponent}
465	  \uses{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso}
466	  Given the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij} : f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i \xrightarrow{\sim}
467	  (t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), the \(j\)-th component of the candidate
468	  inverse \(\mathrm{s}_i\) is the composite
469	  \[
470	    \mathcal{M}_i
471	      \xrightarrow{\ \eta_{f_{ij}}\ }
472	    (f_{ij})_{*}\,f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i
473	      \xrightarrow{\ (f_{ij})_{*}g_{ij}\ }
474	    (f_{ij})_{*}\,(t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j
475	      \xrightarrow{\ \gamma_{ij}^{-1}\ }
476	    \iota_i^{*}\bigl((\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\bigr),
477	  \]
478	  i.e. the geometric unit along \(f_{ij}\), the pushforward of the transition
479	  \(g_{ij}\), and the inverse of \(\gamma_{ij}\) (\cref{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso}).
480	\end{definition}
481	
482	\begin{definition}[The candidate-inverse family]
483	  \label{def:gr_glueChartFamily}
484	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueChartFamily}
485	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartComponent, def:gr_glue_equalizer}
486	  Assembling the components \cref{def:gr_glueChartComponent} through the
487	  product-preservation comparison of \(\iota_i^{*}\) on the pushforward product
488	  \(P = \prod_j (\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\) (\cref{def:gr_glue_equalizer}) gives a
489	  single morphism
490	  \[
491	    \mathcal{M}_i \;\longrightarrow\; \iota_i^{*}P,
492	  \]
493	  the candidate inverse before it is corrected to land in the equalizer
494	  \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\).
495	\end{definition}
496	
497	\begin{lemma}[The candidate-inverse family equalizes the restricted legs (C2 transported)]
498	  \label{lem:gr_glueChartFamily_equalizes}
499	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueChartFamily_equalizes}
500	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartFamily, def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
501	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_overlap_appIso_compat,
502	    lem:gr_glueChartComponent_self_counit, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
503	  The family \cref{def:gr_glueChartFamily} equalizes the two restricted descent legs
504	  \(\iota_i^{*}a\) and \(\iota_i^{*}b\); equivalently, it lands in the restricted
505	  equalizer \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\). This is the first descent obligation, where the
506	  triple-overlap multiplicativity (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} is consumed.
507	\end{lemma}
508	\begin{proof}
509	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartFamily, def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
510	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_overlap_appIso_compat,
511	    lem:gr_glueChartComponent_self_counit}
512	  It suffices to check equality after projecting to each factor \((p,q)\) of the
513	  restricted overlap product, through the product-preservation comparison of
514	  \(\iota_i^{*}\) (the same comparison discipline as \cref{lem:gr_glueRestrict_proj_compat});
515	  the \((p,q)\)-component is the equation
516	  \[
517	    \mathrm{s}_i^{(p)}\ \circ\ \iota_i^{*}\bigl(a_{(p,q)}\bigr)
518	      \;=\;
519	    \mathrm{s}_i^{(q)}\ \circ\ \iota_i^{*}\bigl(b_{(p,q)}\bigr)
520	  \]
521	  between morphisms \(\mathcal{M}_i \to \iota_i^{*}\bigl((f_{pq}\circ \iota_p)_{*}
522	  f_{pq}^{*}\mathcal{M}_p\bigr)\). Transpose this equation along the open immersion
523	  \(q_{pq} : V_{ipq} \hookrightarrow U_i\) of the triple overlap \(V_{ipq} = V_{ip}
524	  \times_{U_i} V_{iq}\) (with \(q_{pq}\) the first projection followed by \(f_{ip}\)),
525	  exactly as the pair-level transpose of \cref{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_mate}. The
526	  transpose requires the triple-overlap analogues of the pair-level toolkit:
527	  \begin{enumerate}
528	    \item the opens identity \((f_{pq}\circ \iota_p)^{-1}(\iota_i(V)) =
529	      \tau\bigl(q_{pq}^{-1}(V)\bigr)\) on \(V_{ipq}\), where \(\tau\) is the base-change
530	      transport morphism, obtained from the pullback square, the cocycle-transport
531	      factorisation, and the gluing-incidence criterion exactly as the pair-level
532	      opens identity of \cref{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso};
533	    \item a triple overlap base change \((f_{pq}\circ \iota_p)_{*}\circ \iota_i^{*}
534	      \cong \tau^{*}\circ (q_{pq})_{*}\), assembled by the same four-factor recipe
535	      (pushforward composition, transport of the opens functor, the congruence factor,
536	      and the inverse pushforward composition) over (1), with the section-level
537	      compatibility proved by the \(\mathrm{appLE}\)-calculus pattern of
538	      \cref{lem:gr_overlap_appIso_compat};
539	    \item with (1) and (2) in place, the transposed component equation \emph{is} the
540	      cocycle multiplicativity (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} at the triple
541	      \((i,p,q)\), conjugated by the base-change transports
542	      \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}; the geometric bridges
543	      \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges} were chosen so that the endpoints of the two sides
544	      line up on the nose.
545	  \end{enumerate}
546	  The degenerate pairs \(p = i\) or \(q = i\) instead invoke the self-identity (C1)
547	  through \cref{lem:gr_glueChartComponent_self_counit} and the congruence cast
548	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom}.
549	\end{proof}

551	\begin{definition}[The candidate inverse \(\mathrm{s}_i\)]
552	  \label{def:gr_glueRestrictionInv}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionInv}
554	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartFamily, lem:gr_glueChartFamily_equalizes}
555	  Because \(\iota_i^{*}\) preserves the descent equalizer, the equalizing family
556	  \cref{def:gr_glueChartFamily} (equalizing by \cref{lem:gr_glueChartFamily_equalizes})
557	  lifts uniquely to a morphism
558	  \[
559	    \mathrm{s}_i \;:\; \mathcal{M}_i \;\longrightarrow\; \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M},
560	  \]
561	  the candidate inverse of the chart restriction morphism.
562	\end{definition}
563	
564	\begin{lemma}[Comparison compatibility of the restricted projection]
565	  \label{lem:gr_glueRestrict_proj_compat}
566	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrict_proj_compat}
567	  \uses{def:gr_glue_equalizer, def:scheme_modules_glue}
568	  Through the equalizer- and product-preservation comparisons of \(\iota_i^{*}\), the
569	  restricted equalizer inclusion of \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) into \(\iota_i^{*}P\)
570	  followed by the \(j\)-th product projection equals the chart pullback
571	  \(\iota_i^{*}(\pi_j)\) of the \(j\)-th descent-equalizer projection
572	  (\cref{def:gr_glue_equalizer}).
573	\end{lemma}
574	\begin{proof}
575	  \uses{def:gr_glue_equalizer}
576	  Pure limit bookkeeping: the equalizer- and product-comparison isomorphisms
577	  associated to a limit-preserving functor send the restricted inclusion and the
578	  restricted projection to the pullbacks of the inclusion and the projection
579	  respectively, and the composite of pullbacks is the pullback of the composite, which
580	  is \(\iota_i^{*}(\pi_j)\).
581	\end{proof}
582	
583	\begin{lemma}[The candidate inverse recovers the components]
584	  \label{lem:gr_glueRestrictionInv_proj}
585	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionInv_pullback_map_glueProj}
586	  \uses{def:gr_glueRestrictionInv, lem:gr_glueRestrict_proj_compat,
587	    def:gr_glueChartComponent}
588	  The candidate inverse \cref{def:gr_glueRestrictionInv} followed by the restricted
589	  \(j\)-th projection \(\iota_i^{*}(\pi_j)\) recovers the \(j\)-th component
590	  \cref{def:gr_glueChartComponent}:
591	  \[
592	    \mathrm{s}_i\ \circ\ \iota_i^{*}(\pi_j)
593	      \;=\; \mathrm{s}_i^{(j)} .
594	  \]
595	\end{lemma}
596	\begin{proof}
597	  \uses{def:gr_glueRestrictionInv, lem:gr_glueRestrict_proj_compat}
598	  Unfolding the lift \cref{def:gr_glueRestrictionInv} and applying
599	  \cref{lem:gr_glueRestrict_proj_compat}, the equalizer- and product-preservation
600	  comparisons cancel against the lift, leaving the defining \(j\)-th projection of the
601	  family \cref{def:gr_glueChartFamily}, namely \(\mathrm{s}_i^{(j)}\).
602	\end{proof}

604	\begin{lemma}[Joint detection of morphisms into the restricted glued sheaf]
605	  \label{lem:gr_glueRestrict_hom_ext}
606	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrict_hom_ext}
607	  \uses{lem:gr_glueRestrict_proj_compat}
608	  Two morphisms \(u, v : \mathcal{Z} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) that agree after every
609	  restricted projection \(\iota_i^{*}(\pi_j)\) are equal.
610	\end{lemma}
611	\begin{proof}
612	  \uses{lem:gr_glueRestrict_proj_compat}
613	  The restricted equalizer inclusion is a monomorphism and the restricted product
614	  projections are jointly monic, both through the preservation comparisons; so it
615	  suffices to test against \(\iota_i^{*}(\pi_j)\) for every \(j\), which is the
616	  hypothesis via \cref{lem:gr_glueRestrict_proj_compat}.
617	\end{proof}

619	\subsection{The three cocycle obligations}
620	
621	\begin{lemma}[Self-component collapses to the identity (C1 + counit triangle)]
622	  \label{lem:gr_glueChartComponent_self_counit}
623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueChartComponent_self_counit}
624	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartComponent, def:scheme_modules_glue,
625	    lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_congr, lem:gr_restrictAdjunction_unit_iso,
626	    lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_counit, lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_inv_app,
627	    lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom}
628	  The \((i,i)\)-component \cref{def:gr_glueChartComponent}, transposed back along the
629	  chart immersion \(\iota_i\) (post-composed with the geometric counit), is the
630	  identity of \(\mathcal{M}_i\):
631	  \[
632	    \mathrm{s}_i^{(i)}\ \circ\ \varepsilon^{\mathrm{geo}}_{\mathcal{M}_i}
633	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{M}_i}.
634	  \]
635	  This is the second descent obligation, consuming the self-identity (C1).
636	\end{lemma}
637	\begin{proof}
638	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartComponent, def:scheme_modules_glue,
639	    lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_congr, lem:gr_restrictAdjunction_unit_iso,
640	    lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_counit, lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_inv_app,
641	    lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom}
642	  Since \(f_{ii}\) factors as \(t_{ii}\circ f_{ii}\) (because \(t_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\)),
643	  (C1) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} says the transition \(g_{ii}\) is the
644	  canonical congruence cast; through \cref{lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom} and
645	  \cref{lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_congr} the three middle factors of the component
646	  collapse to a single site-level congruence cast. Rewriting the geometric unit by
647	  \cref{lem:gr_restrictAdjunction_unit_iso} and the geometric counit by
648	  \cref{lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_counit} turns the whole composite into the
649	  site-level unit, the congruence cast, the section form of \(\beta_{ii}^{-1}\)
650	  (\cref{lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_inv_app}), and the site-level counit. On sections
651	  over an open this is a cycle of presheaf restrictions along opens identities whose
652	  composite open-inclusion is the identity; folding the restrictions and using
653	  proof-irrelevance of the open inclusions gives the identity.
654	\end{proof}
655	
656	\begin{lemma}[Transpose of the overlap base change (mate core)]
657	  \label{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_transpose}
658	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueOverlapFactor_transpose}
659	  \uses{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso, lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso,
660	    lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_hom_app, def:scheme_modules_glue}
661	  The adjoint transpose along \(\iota_i\) of the pullback-form overlap base change
662	  \(\gamma_{ij}\) (\cref{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso}) is the canonical four-functor
663	  comparison of the glue square:
664	  \[
665	    \widehat{\gamma_{ij}}
666	      \;=\;
667	    (\iota_j)_{*}\bigl(\eta_{t_{ij}\circ f_{ji}}\bigr)
668	      \ \circ\ \mathrm{pushComp}
669	      \ \circ\ \mathrm{pushCongr}
670	      \ \circ\ \mathrm{pushComp}^{-1},
671	  \]
672	  the unit along \(t_{ij}\circ f_{ji}\) pushed forward along \(\iota_j\), regrouped by
673	  the pushforward-composition comparison, reindexed by the pushforward-congruence along
674	  the glue condition \((t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})\circ \iota_j = f_{ij}\circ \iota_i\), and
675	  ungrouped by the inverse pushforward-composition. The identity is free of the
676	  cocycle hypotheses: it is pure site-level content.
677	\end{lemma}
678	\begin{proof}
679	  \uses{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso, lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso,
680	    lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_hom_app, def:scheme_modules_glue}
681	  Expand the adjoint transpose of \(\gamma_{ij}\): the left-adjoint comparison
682	  isomorphisms (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}) conjugating the
683	  chart factor cancel against the unit/counit, leaving the bare site-level overlap base
684	  change \(\beta_{ij}\) (\cref{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}). The unit bridge at
685	  \(t_{ij}\circ f_{ji}\) and the three naturality squares of the pushforward-composition
686	  and pushforward-congruence comparisons rewrite the transpose into the stated
687	  four-factor composite up to whiskering and reassociation. The residual site-level
688	  core is checked on sections over an open: by \cref{lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_hom_app}
689	  the section form of \(\beta_{ij}\) is a presheaf restriction along an opens identity,
690	  and the remaining composite is a cycle of such restrictions whose total open
691	  inclusion is the identity, folded by proof-irrelevance exactly as in the closing step
692	  of \cref{lem:gr_glueChartComponent_self_counit}.
693	\end{proof}
694	
695	\begin{lemma}[Mate recognition of the factor transpose]
696	  \label{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_mate}
697	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueOverlapFactor_mate}
698	  \uses{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_transpose, def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso,
699	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
700	  For any morphism \(m : \mathcal{W} \to (\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\) on the glued
701	  scheme, the conjugated transport of the inverse transpose of \(m\) along the glue
702	  square equals the inverse transpose, along \(f_{ij}\), of \(\iota_i^{*}(m)\) followed
703	  by \(\gamma_{ij}\) (\cref{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso}). In other words, the factor
704	  transpose \cref{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_transpose} is the mate of \(\gamma_{ij}\)
705	  through the pseudofunctor structure of pullback.
706	\end{lemma}
707	\begin{proof}
708	  \uses{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_transpose, def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso,
709	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
710	  Both sides are computed by transposing twice. By injectivity of the two transpose
711	  bijections it suffices to compare the double transposes. Naturality of the transpose
712	  in its left argument peels \(m\) off the right-hand side; the conjugate of the
713	  pullback-composition isomorphism along the glue condition is the inverse
714	  pushforward-composition isomorphism; and folding the unit pair and reindexing along
715	  the glue condition of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} reduces the identity to the
716	  factor transpose \cref{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_transpose}.
717	\end{proof}
718	
719	\begin{lemma}[Overlap compatibility of the restriction morphisms]
720	  \label{lem:gr_glueRestriction_overlap_compat}
721	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestriction_overlap_compat}
722	  \uses{lem:glueRestrictionHom, def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_glue_equalizer}

724	  pullback level, states that over the overlap \(V_{ij}\) the two restriction
725	  morphisms \(\mathrm{r}_i\) and \(\mathrm{r}_j\) (\cref{lem:glueRestrictionHom}) differ
726	  exactly by the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) (through the pseudofunctor

729	\begin{proof}
730	  \uses{lem:glueRestrictionHom, def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_glue_equalizer}

732	  the overlap-agreement condition of \cref{def:gr_glue_equalizer}. Transposing that
733	  condition along \(\iota_i\) and \(\iota_j\) and folding the transposes back through
734	  the adjunction bijection turns it into the stated identity between the overlap
735	  pullbacks of \(\mathrm{r}_i\) and \(\mathrm{r}_j\), conjugated by the glue-condition
736	  casts of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} and corrected by \(g_{ij}\).
737	\end{proof}

739	\begin{lemma}[Pair condition transposed: restriction composed with the component]
740	  \label{lem:gr_glueRestrictionHom_glueChartComponent}
741	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionHom_glueChartComponent}
742	  \uses{lem:gr_glueRestriction_overlap_compat, lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_mate,
743	    def:gr_glueChartComponent, lem:glueRestrictionHom}
744	  The chart restriction morphism \(\mathrm{r}_i\) (\cref{lem:glueRestrictionHom})
745	  followed by the \(j\)-th candidate-inverse component \cref{def:gr_glueChartComponent}

747	  \[
748	    \mathrm{r}_i\ \circ\ \mathrm{s}_i^{(j)}
749	      \;=\; \iota_i^{*}(\pi_j).
750	  \]
751	  This is the third descent obligation; together with
752	  \cref{lem:gr_glueRestrictionInv_proj} and \cref{lem:gr_glueRestrict_hom_ext} it gives
753	  \(\mathrm{r}_i \circ \mathrm{s}_i = \mathrm{id}\).
754	\end{lemma}
755	\begin{proof}
756	  \uses{lem:gr_glueRestriction_overlap_compat, lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_mate,
757	    def:gr_glueChartComponent, lem:glueRestrictionHom}
758	  Unfold the component \cref{def:gr_glueChartComponent}. By the overlap compatibility
759	  \cref{lem:gr_glueRestriction_overlap_compat} the overlap pullback of \(\mathrm{r}_i\)
760	  transported across \(g_{ij}\) is the inverse mate transpose of the restricted
761	  projection \(\iota_i^{*}(\pi_j)\) through \(\gamma_{ij}\)
762	  (\cref{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_mate}). Substituting this into the unit-naturality

767	\subsection{Joint faithfulness of chart restrictions}
768	
769	\begin{lemma}[Chart restrictions are jointly faithful]
770	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_jointly_faithful}
771	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_map_jointly_faithful}
772	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib,
773	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
774	  Two morphisms \(u, v : \mathcal{W} \to \mathcal{W}'\) of sheaves of modules on the
775	  glued scheme that agree after pullback to every chart, \(\iota_i^{*}u = \iota_i^{*}v\)
776	  for all \(i\), are equal. This realises the restriction-uniqueness core of the
777	  characterisation of the glued sheaf up to unique isomorphism: a morphism out of the
778	  glued sheaf is determined chart-locally.
779	\end{lemma}
780	\begin{proof}
781	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
782	  Transfer the chart agreement to the site-level restriction functor by conjugating
783	  with the comparison \cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}, whose
784	  sections are concrete: \(\Gamma(\iota_i^{\mathrm{res}}\mathcal{W}, V) =
785	  \Gamma(\mathcal{W}, \iota_i(V))\). Then test \(u\) and \(v\) on sections over any
786	  open \(O\) of the glued scheme using separatedness of the target sheaf
787	  (\cref{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}) over the cover \(\{\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}O)\}\)
788	  of \(O\) — which is a cover by joint surjectivity of the chart immersions
789	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}). On each cover piece the agreement is the chart
790	  identity moved inside the sections by naturality of \(u, v\) on the presheaf level.
791	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}\leanok
77	[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
78	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
79	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport}
80	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
81	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
82	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
83	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
84	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
85	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
86	  \[
87	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
88	  \]
89	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
90	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
91	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
92	  transition, satisfying
93	  \[
94	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
95	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
96	  \]
97	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
100	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
101	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
102	  \]
103	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
104	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
105	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
106	  \[
107	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
108	      \;:\;
109	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
110	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
111	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
112	  \]
113	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
114	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
115	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
116	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
117	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
118	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
119	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
120	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
125	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
126	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
127	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
128	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
129	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
130	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
131	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
132	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
133	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
134	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
135	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
136	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
137	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
138	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
139	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
140	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
141	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
142	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
143	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
144	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
145	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
146	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
147	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
148	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
149	\end{proof}
150	
151	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap endpoint bridges]
152	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
154	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
155	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
156	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
157	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) and fix
158	  indices \(i,j,k\) with triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\).
159	  The three transports of \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} have
160	  endpoints pinned down by three scheme-morphism equalities on \(V_{ijk}\), each a
161	  pure consequence of the glue datum's structural identities:
162	  \begin{itemize}
163	    \item (\emph{src}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ f_{ij} = \mathrm{snd} \circ f_{ik}\)
164	      -- the defining pullback condition of \(V_{ijk}\);
165	    \item (\emph{mid}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})
166	      = (t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ f_{jk}\) -- the pullback condition
167	      composed with the glue datum's \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identity;
168	    \item (\emph{tgt}) \((t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ (t_{jk} \circ f_{kj})
169	      = \mathrm{snd} \circ (t_{ik} \circ f_{ki})\) -- the cocycle core, from the
170	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) associativity, the reversed pullback condition, the
171	      \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\) collapse and the glue datum's cocycle identity.
172	  \end{itemize}
173	  These three equalities align the source, middle, and target sheaves of the three
174	  transports so that the cocycle equation (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}
175	  typechecks.
176	\end{lemma}
177	\begin{proof}  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
178	  Each equality is an identity of morphisms in the category of schemes, proved by
179	  rewriting with the glue datum's axioms: \emph{src} is literally
180	  \(\mathrm{pullback.condition}\); \emph{mid} adds the \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)
181	  factorisation \(D.t_{\mathrm{fac}}\,i\,j\,k\); \emph{tgt} chains
182	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at \((j,k,i)\), the reversed pullback
183	  condition, an auxiliary collapse combining \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at
184	  \((k,i,j)\) with \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\), and finally the cocycle identity
185	  \(D.\mathrm{cocycle}\,i\,j\,k\). No sheaf-of-modules data enters; these are
186	  bookkeeping equalities of the underlying scheme cover.
187	\end{proof}
188	
189	% HISTORICAL (abandoned route): an earlier draft built the glued sheaf as a hand-rolled
190	% presheaf of compatible families (decls gluePresheaf / gluePresheafModule / gluePresheafIsSheaf).
191	% That route is abandoned; the gluing is now the equalizer of pushforwards described in the
192	% Construction paragraph of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}, which subsumes it.
193	
194	\begin{definition}
195	
196	\leanok
197	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
198	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
199	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
200	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:modules_pullbackComp,
201	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
202	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
203	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
204	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
205	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
206	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
207	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
208	  charts. Suppose given:
209	  \begin{itemize}
210	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
211	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
212	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
213	      \[
214	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
215	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
216	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
217	      \]
218	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
219	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
220	  \end{itemize}
221	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
222	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
223	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
224	  \((g_{ij})\):
225	  \begin{enumerate}
226	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
227	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
228	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
229	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
230	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
231	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
232	      isomorphisms satisfy
233	      \[
234	        \widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
235	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
236	      \]
237	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
238	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
239	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
240	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
241	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
242	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
243	      condition.
244	  \end{enumerate}
245	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
246	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing is well-defined. Out of this
247	  data the construction produces a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
248	  \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\).
249	
250	  \emph{Construction.} The category \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of sheaves of
251	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has all limits, and both pushforward along a morphism and
252	  these limits preserve the sheaf condition; the glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is therefore
253	  built directly as a limit -- an \emph{equalizer of pushforwards} -- rather than through a
254	  hand-built presheaf of compatible families. Concretely, form the two parallel maps of
255	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
256	  \[
257	    \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
258	      \;\;\underset{b}{\overset{a}{\rightrightarrows}}\;\;
259	    \prod_{i,j} (\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}\,\bigl(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\bigr),
260	  \]
261	  where, on the \((i,j)\)-component, leg \(a\) pushes the \(i\)-th factor forward along the
262	  overlap immersion \(f_{ij}\) using the unit of the pullback--pushforward adjunction
263	  together with the pushforward-composition comparison
264	  \((\iota_i)_{*} \cong (\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}\circ f_{ij}^{*}\) (the dual of
265	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), and leg \(b\) pushes the \(j\)-th factor forward and
266	  additionally transports it across the inverse transition isomorphism \((g_{ij})^{-1}\)
267	  and the glue datum's compatibility identity, so that both legs land on the common overlap
268	  sheaf \((\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i)\). The glued sheaf is the
269	  equalizer
270	  \[
271	    \mathcal{M} \;=\; \operatorname{eq}(a,b) \;\hookrightarrow\; \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i,
272	  \]
273	  the sub-sheaf of tuples of pushed-forward chart sections whose two overlap restrictions
274	  agree -- precisely the compatible families, now realised as a categorical limit rather than
275	  a bespoke presheaf. The cocycle conditions (C1)/(C2) on the family \((g_{ij})\) are
276	  \emph{not} consumed in forming this equalizer object: the limit exists for any family of
277	  transition maps. They are instead what make the equalizer descend correctly, and are
278	  exactly the hypotheses pinned down downstream when the restriction isomorphisms
279	  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso} are produced. This specialises effective descent
280	  of sheaves of modules to the concrete open cover \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\)
281	  supplied by a scheme glue datum, and is the form in which the Grassmannian's
282	  tautological bundle and quotient are built.
283	
284	  The glued sheaf comes with three further structures, each recorded as its own block
285	  downstream: the \emph{restriction isomorphisms} \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}
286	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps
287	  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}; the \emph{characterisation up to unique
288	  isomorphism} by the restriction property \cref{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}; and the
289	  \emph{descent of morphisms} -- a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
290	  commuting with the transition isomorphisms glues to a unique \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to
291	  \mathcal{N}\) with \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ
292	  \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\) \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}.
293	\end{definition}
294	
295	\begin{definition}[Restriction isomorphisms of the glued sheaf]
296	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}
297	  % NOTE: the Lean decl `glueRestrictionIso` is pinned canonically by
298	  % `def:glueRestrictionIso` in Picard_GlueDescent.tex (its 1:1 home after the
299	  % iter-067 file split). This block is the downstream consumer's cross-reference
300	  % node; it carries no \lean{} pin to avoid a duplicate.
301	  \uses{def:glueRestrictionIso, def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
302	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
303	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be the glued sheaf of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. For each
304	  chart index \(i\) there is a \emph{restriction isomorphism} of sheaves of
305	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
306	  \[
307	    \rho_i \;:\; \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{M}_i,
308	  \]
309	  compatible with the transition isomorphisms: over each overlap \(V_{ij}\) the two
310	  restrictions \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\), transported to the common overlap, differ
311	  exactly by \(g_{ij}\).
312	\end{definition}
313	\begin{proof}
314	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
315	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
316	  By the equalizer-of-pushforwards description \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}, a section of
317	  \(\mathcal{M}\) over an open of the form \(\iota_i^{-1}V\), restricted to the chart
318	  \(U_i\), is recovered from and recovers its \(i\)-th component
319	  \(s_i \in \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \cdot)\) -- the \(i\)-th factor of the product
320	  \(\prod_i (\iota_i)_{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) into which the equalizer embeds; the
321	  chart-restriction bridge \cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso} identifies the pullback
322	  \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) with this restriction, and the \(i\)-th projection
323	  \((s_\bullet) \mapsto s_i\) is the isomorphism \(\rho_i\) (with inverse extending a chart
324	  section by the overlap relation).
325	  The overlap compatibility is the defining relation of the compatible families: over
326	  \(V_{ij}\) the components \(s_i, s_j\) are related by \(g_{ij}\), which is precisely the
327	  statement that \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\) differ by \(g_{ij}\) after transport to
328	  \(V_{ij}\); the triple-overlap coherence needed for this to be consistent is (C2),
329	  whose transports are aligned by \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} and
330	  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}.
331	\end{proof}
332	
333	\begin{lemma}[Uniqueness of the glued sheaf up to canonical isomorphism]
334	  \label{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}
335	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue_unique}
336	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
337	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
338	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
339	  Suppose \(\mathcal{M}'\) is another sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules equipped with
340	  isomorphisms \(\rho'_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}' \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
341	  compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps. Then there is a unique isomorphism
342	  \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) intertwining the restriction
343	  isomorphisms, i.e.\ with \(\rho'_i \circ \iota_i^{*}(\cdot) = \rho_i\) for every \(i\).
344	\end{lemma}
345	\begin{proof}
346	  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
347	  A morphism of sheaves of modules on \(X\) is determined by, and may be assembled from,
348	  its restrictions to the members of the open cover \(\{\iota_i\}\), agreeing on the
349	  overlaps -- the locality and gluing of sections
350	  (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom. The
351	  intertwining condition forces the restriction over \(U_i\) to be
352	  \((\rho'_i)^{-1} \circ \rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}'\);
353	  these local isomorphisms agree on each overlap \(V_{ij}\) because both
354	  \((\rho_i)\) and \((\rho'_i)\) are compatible with the \emph{same} transition family
355	  \((g_{ij})\) (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}). By locality they glue to a
356	  unique global isomorphism \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) with the
357	  required property; uniqueness is the separation half of the same gluing.
358	\end{proof}
359	
360	\begin{definition}[Descent of morphisms along the cover]
361	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueHom}
362	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueHom}
363	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
364	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
365	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
366	  Let \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) be two glued data
367	  over the same glue datum \(D\), with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\) and
368	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i, \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i\). Given a family
369	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
370	  commuting with the transition isomorphisms on every overlap (i.e.\ \(h_{ij}\) intertwines
371	  the pullbacks of \(\phi_i\) and \(\phi_j\) over \(V_{ij}\)), there is a unique morphism
372	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) with
373	  \[
374	    \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi \;=\; \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i
375	    \qquad \text{for every } i.
376	  \]
377	\end{definition}
378	\begin{proof}
379	  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
380	  On each chart the prescribed identity defines a local morphism
381	  \((\rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i)^{-1} \circ \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i :
382	  \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{N}\). The commutation of the \(\phi_i\)
383	  with the transition isomorphisms \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\), together with the overlap
384	  compatibility of the \(\rho\)-families (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}), makes
385	  these local morphisms agree on every overlap \(V_{ij}\). By the locality and gluing of
386	  sections (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom they
387	  promote to a unique global morphism \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) restricting
388	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the
389	  cover.
390	\end{proof}
