392	\subsection{Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary morphism}
393	\label{sec:grquot_pullback_free}
394	
395	The functor of points (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}) acts on morphisms by pullback, so
396	it needs the pullback of the trivial bundle to be again trivial --
397	\(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}\) -- for an
398	\emph{arbitrary} scheme morphism \(\varphi\). Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison applies
399	only when the underlying site functor \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is \emph{final}; the
400	following three blocks remove that restriction for every morphism, and the resulting
401	pullback machinery preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness.
402	
403	\begin{lemma}\leanok
404	[The opens-pullback functor is final]
405	  \label{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
406	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensMap_final}
407	  For any morphism of schemes \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the inverse-image functor on opens
408	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi : \mathrm{Opens}(T) \to \mathrm{Opens}(T')\),
409	  \(U \mapsto \varphi^{-1}U\), is \emph{final}.
410	\end{lemma}
411	
412	\begin{proof}
413	
414	  Finality means that for every open \(V \subseteq T'\) the structured-arrow category of
415	  opens \(U \subseteq T\) with \(V \le \varphi^{-1}U\) is connected. That category is
416	  nonempty and has the terminal object \(U = \top\) -- one always has
417	  \(V \le \varphi^{-1}\top = T'\), and every object admits a unique arrow to \(\top\) --
418	  and a category with a terminal object is connected.
419	\end{proof}
420	
421	\begin{lemma}\leanok
422	[Pullback of a free sheaf is free]
423	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
424	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso}
425	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
426	  For any scheme morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), there is a canonical
427	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{T'}\)-modules
428	  \[
429	    \varphi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\bigr)
430	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
431	    \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}.
432	  \]
433	\end{lemma}
434	
435	\begin{proof}
436	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
437	  Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison produces this isomorphism whenever the site functor
438	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is final; that finality is \cref{lem:gr_opensMap_final},
439	  which holds for every morphism, so the comparison applies unconditionally.
440	\end{proof}
441	
442	\begin{lemma}
443	
444	\leanok
445	[Free-pullback comparison is natural in the base morphism]
446	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_eqToHom}
447	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_eqToHom}
448	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
449	  Let \(\varphi, \psi : T' \to T\) be morphisms with \(\varphi = \psi\) and \(I\) an index
450	  type. The two free-pullback comparisons \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} along \(\varphi\) and
451	  \(\psi\) agree after transporting the differing pullback sources by the canonical
452	  \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\): the composite of that transport with
453	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\psi)\) equals \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi)\).
454	\end{lemma}
455	\begin{proof}
456	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
457	  Substituting the equality \(\varphi = \psi\) collapses the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) to the
458	  identity and the two comparisons coincide.
459	\end{proof}
460	
461	\begin{lemma}
462	
463	\leanok
464	[Iso-level free-pullback cancellation]
465	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso}
466	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso}
467	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
468	  For equal base morphisms \(\varphi = \psi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), the composite
469	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi) \circ \mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\psi)^{-1}\) is the
470	  \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) transport between the (differing) pullback sources of the free sheaf
471	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\). Stated and proved generically in the base morphisms, so its
472	  use never forces evaluation of a concrete immersion; this is the load-bearing replacement
473	  for the cast chain in the self-identity \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id}.
474	\end{lemma}
475	\begin{proof}
476	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
477	  Substituting \(\varphi = \psi\) turns the composite into
478	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi) \circ \mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi)^{-1} =
479	  \mathrm{id}\), which is the \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) of the now-trivial source equality.
480	\end{proof}
481	
482	\begin{lemma}\leanok
483	[Pullback preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness]
484	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
485	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
486	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
487	  Let \(\varphi : T' \to T\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
488	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules locally free of rank \(d\)
489	  (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}). Then the pullback \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is
490	  locally free of rank \(d\) on \(T'\).
491	\end{lemma}
492	
493	\begin{proof}
494	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
495	  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(T\) trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\),
496	  \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\). The preimages
497	  \(\{\varphi^{-1}U_i\}\) cover \(T'\). On each member the factorisation
498	  \(\varphi \circ \iota_{\varphi^{-1}U_i} = (\varphi|_{U_i}) \circ \iota_{U_i}\) of the
499	  restricted morphism, together with the pseudofunctor comparison of iterated pullbacks
500	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), identifies
501	  \((\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M})|_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}\) with
502	  \((\varphi|_{U_i})^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\); the latter is
503	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}^{\,d}\) by \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. Hence
504	  \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is locally free of rank \(d\).
505	\end{proof}
506	
507	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
508	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
509	
510	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
511	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
512	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
513	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
514	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
515	
516	\begin{definition}\leanok
517	[Global section of the unit module]
518	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
519	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
520	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
521	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
522	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
523	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
524	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
525	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
526	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
527	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
528	  global function.
529	\end{definition}
530	
531	\begin{definition}\leanok
532	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
533	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
534	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
535	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
536	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
537	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
538	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
539	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
540	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
541	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
542	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
543	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
544	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
545	\end{definition}
546	
547	\begin{lemma}\leanok
548	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
549	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
550	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
551	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
552	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
553	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
554	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
555	\end{lemma}
556	
557	\begin{proof}
558	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
559	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
560	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
561	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
562	\end{proof}
563	
564	\begin{lemma}\leanok
565	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
566	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
567	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
568	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
569	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
570	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
571	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
572	\end{lemma}
573	
574	\begin{proof}
575	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
576	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
577	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
578	\end{proof}
579	
580	\begin{lemma}\leanok
581	[Composition of scalar endomorphisms is multiplication]
582	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}
583	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_comp}
584	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
585	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the scalar endomorphisms compose by
586	  multiplication: \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) \circ \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) =
587	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a\,b)\).
588	\end{lemma}
589	\begin{proof}
590	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
591	  Under the identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the
592	  scalar endomorphisms act as multiplication by \(a\) and by \(b\); their composite
593	  is multiplication by \(a\,b\), because the section ring is commutative and
594	  restriction maps are ring homomorphisms.
595	\end{proof}
596	
597	\begin{lemma}\leanok
598	[Scalar endomorphism is additive]
599	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
600	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_add}
601	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
602	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) one has \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a + b) =
603	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) + \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b)\).
604	\end{lemma}
605	\begin{proof}
606	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
607	  Multiplication by \(a + b\) equals multiplication by \(a\) plus multiplication by
608	  \(b\) on every section, since the restriction maps of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) are additive;
609	  under \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) this is the stated additivity.
610	\end{proof}
611	
612	\begin{lemma}
613	
614	\leanok
615	[Scalar endomorphism of a finite sum]
616	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_sum}
617	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_sum}
618	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
619	  For a finite index set \(s\) and a family \(f : \iota \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\),
620	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(\sum_{i \in s} f_i\bigr) = \sum_{i \in s} \mathrm{scalarEnd}(f_i)\).
621	\end{lemma}
622	\begin{proof}
623	  \uses{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
624	  Induction on the finite set \(s\): the empty case is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
625	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}) and the insertion step is additivity
626	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}).
627	\end{proof}
628	
629	\emph{Infrastructure: matrix automorphisms of the free sheaf.} To realise the
630	\(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transitions one must promote an invertible \(d \times d\)
631	matrix of global functions to an \emph{automorphism} of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf
632	\(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\), exactly as the chart quotient \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}
633	realises the universal matrix. The next blocks assemble this matrix-to-morphism
634	functor and record its two structural identities -- that it carries matrix
635	multiplication to composition and the identity matrix to the identity -- which are
636	the algebraic heart of the bundle cocycle.
637	
638	\begin{definition}\leanok
639	[Matrix endomorphism of the free sheaf]
640	  \label{def:gr_matrixEnd}
641	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd}
642	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
643	  Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), and
644	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) a matrix of
645	  global functions. The \emph{matrix endomorphism} \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is the
646	  endomorphism of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) whose
647	  \((p,q)\)-component, under the presentation of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) as the
648	  finite biproduct of \(d\) copies of the unit module \(\mathbf{1}\), is the scalar
649	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{p,q})\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}). It is
650	  assembled from these components by the universal property of the rank-\(d\)
651	  biproduct, exactly as the chart quotient \(u^I\) is assembled
652	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). This is the substitute for the (non-existent)
653	  Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) endomorphism of a free sheaf'' primitive.
654	\end{definition}
655	
656	\begin{lemma}\leanok
657	[Matrix endomorphism carries multiplication to composition]
658	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
659	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_comp}
660	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
661	  For \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) the matrix
662	  endomorphisms compose according to the matrix product, with the order reversed by
663	  the contravariance of the component indexing:
664	  \[
665	    \mathrm{matrixEnd}(M) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)
666	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N \cdot M).
667	  \]
668	\end{lemma}
669	\begin{proof}
670	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
671	  The composition of two biproduct-assembled morphisms is again biproduct-assembled,
672	  with \((i,q)\)-component the sum over the middle index \(p\) of the composites of
673	  components, \(\sum_p \mathrm{component}_{i,p} \circ \mathrm{component}_{p,q}\) (the
674	  categorical matrix product over the biproduct). Each composite of scalar
675	  endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the product,
676	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\circ\mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(ab)\)
677	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}), and a finite sum of scalar endomorphisms is the
678	  scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}); hence the \((i,q)\)-component
679	  of the composite is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(\sum_p M_{q,p} N_{p,i}\bigr) =
680	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl((N\cdot M)_{q,i}\bigr)\), which is precisely the
681	  \((i,q)\)-component of \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N\cdot M)\). The reversal of the factor
682	  order is the contravariance of the column/component indexing.
683	\end{proof}
684	
685	\begin{lemma}\leanok
686	[Matrix endomorphism of the identity is the identity]
687	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
688	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_one}
689	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
690	  The matrix endomorphism of the identity matrix is the identity morphism of
691	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\): \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(1_{d\times d}) =
692	  \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}}\).
693	\end{lemma}
694	\begin{proof}
695	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
696	  The diagonal components are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\)
697	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the off-diagonal components are
698	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the morphism with this
699	  component matrix is the identity of the biproduct.
700	\end{proof}
701	
702	\begin{definition}\leanok
703	[Free-sheaf automorphism of an invertible matrix]
704	  \label{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}
705	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso}
706	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp, lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
707	  Let \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) be mutually
708	  inverse, \(M N = 1\) and \(N M = 1\). The \emph{matrix automorphism}
709	  \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is the isomorphism of free sheaves
710	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) with forward map
711	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) and inverse map \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)\); the two
712	  composites are identities by \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp} (turning the products
713	  \(NM\) and \(MN\) into the relevant compositions) and \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}.
714	  Its forward map is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) by construction
715	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}).
716	\end{definition}
717	
718	\begin{lemma}\leanok
719	[Forward map of the matrix automorphism]
720	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}
721	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_hom}
722	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
723	  The forward map of \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
724	\end{lemma}
725	\begin{proof}
726	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
727	  Immediate from the definition \cref{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}, whose forward field is
728	  literally \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
729	\end{proof}
730	
731	\begin{lemma}\leanok
732	[Matrix automorphisms compose multiplicatively]
733	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul}
734	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_mul}
735	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
736	  Let \(A, A', B, B'\) be \(d \times d\) matrices of global functions on \(S\) with
737	  \(A A' = A' A = 1\), \(B B' = B' B = 1\), so that \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')\)
738	  and \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')\) are automorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\).
739	  Their forward maps compose to the forward map of the product automorphism, with the
740	  order reversed by the component contravariance:
741	  \[
742	    \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')_{\mathrm{hom}}
743	      \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')_{\mathrm{hom}}
744	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B \cdot A).
745	  \]
746	  Equivalently, composition of matrix automorphisms realises matrix multiplication of
747	  the underlying matrices. This is the linkage that turns the matrix-level cocycle
748	  identity into a composition identity of sheaf-of-module isomorphisms.
749	\end{lemma}
750	\begin{proof}
751	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
752	  By \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom} the two forward maps are
753	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\) and \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B)\); their composite is
754	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\circ\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B) = \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B\cdot A)\) by
755	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}.
756	\end{proof}
757	
758	\begin{definition}
759	
760	\leanok
761	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
762	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
763	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
764	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
765	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
766	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
767	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
768	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
769	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
770	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
771	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
772	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
773	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
774	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
775	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
776	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
777	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
778	  \[
779	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
780	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
781	  \]
782	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
783	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
784	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
785	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
786	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
787	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
788	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
789	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
790	
791	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
792	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
793	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
794	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
795	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
796	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
797	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
798	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
799	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
800	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
801	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
802	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
803	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
804	  free sheaves'' primitive.
805	\end{definition}
806	
807	\begin{lemma}\leanok
808	[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
809	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
810	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
811	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
812	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
813	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
814	  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
815	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
816	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
817	  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
818	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
819	  identity:
820	  \[
821	    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
822	  \]
823	\end{lemma}
824	
825	\begin{proof}
826	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
827	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
828	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
829	  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
830	  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
831	  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
832	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
833	  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
834	  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
835	  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
836	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
837	  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
838	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
839	  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
840	\end{proof}
841	
842	\begin{lemma}\leanok
843	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
844	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
845	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
846	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
847	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
848	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
849	\end{lemma}
850	
851	\begin{proof}
852	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
853	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
854	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
855	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
856	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
857	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
858	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
859	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
860	  \[
861	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
862	  \]
863	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
864	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
865	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
866	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
867	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
868	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
869	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
870	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
871	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
872	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
873	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
874	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
875	\end{proof}
876	
877	\section{The \(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transition cocycle}
878	\label{sec:grquot_bundle_cocycle}
879	
880	The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) is
881	obtained by feeding the gluing primitive \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} the per-chart free
882	rank-\(d\) sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\), the bundle transitions
883	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the two module cocycle hypotheses (C1)/(C2) that primitive
884	requires. The matrix cocycle for the Cramer inverses \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) is already in hand --
885	it is the content of the scheme-level cocycle \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} and the minor identities
886	\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities} -- but the gluing primitive consumes its
887	\emph{module-level} incarnation: an honest isomorphism of sheaves of modules together with
888	its self-identity and triple-overlap multiplicativity. The three blocks of this section
889	package exactly that, decoupling the construction of \(\mathcal{U}\) from the remaining
890	matrix-to-module transport bookkeeping. This is the only net-new infrastructure the
891	universal quotient requires beyond the gluing primitive itself.
892	
893	\begin{lemma}
894	
895	\leanok
896	[Self-minor Cramer inverse is the identity]
897	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_self}
898	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_self}
899	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
900	  The Cramer inverse of the self-minor is the identity matrix:
901	  \((X^I_I)^{-1} = 1_{d \times d}\).
902	\end{lemma}
903	\begin{proof}
904	  \uses{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
905	  The self-minor \(X^I_I\) is the identity block
906	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}), and \(1^{-1} = 1\).
907	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{definition}\leanok
117	[Presheaf-of-modules category in monoidal form]
118	  \label{def:monoidalPresheaf}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.MonoidalPresheaf}
120	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
121	  For a scheme \(X\), write \(\PshMod_{\otimes}(X)\) for the category of presheaves
122	  of modules over the underlying presheaf of (plain) rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\),
123	  obtained by composing the structure sheaf with the forgetful functor
124	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\). This is definitionally
125	  \(X.\PshMod\), but presented in the exact form for which Mathlib equips it with
126	  the symmetric monoidal structure of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib};
127	  it is the carrier on which the objectwise tensor product of
128	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} is taken.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\begin{definition}\leanok
132	[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]
133	  \label{def:schemeModuleSheafification}
134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification}
135	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
136	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{module sheafification functor}
137	  \((-)^{\#} : X.\PshMod \to X.\Modules\) is Mathlib's sheafification functor
138	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) specialised to the identity
139	  morphism of the underlying presheaf of rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (a locally
140	  bijective map). Applying it to the objectwise tensor presheaf is what defines the
141	  sheaf tensor product \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}.
142	\end{definition}
143	
144	\begin{definition}\leanok
145	[Unit module of a scheme]
146	  \label{def:unitModule}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitModule}
148	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
149	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{unit module} \(\mathbf{1}_X \in X.\Modules\) is the
150	  structure sheaf viewed as a module over itself, i.e.\ Mathlib's
151	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.unit}\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) for
152	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It is the value of the zeroth tensor power,
153	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}).
154	\end{definition}
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
158	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
160	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
161	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
162	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
163	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
164	  % L2282-L2297).
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
166	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
167	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
168	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
169	  %  $$
170	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
171	  %  $$
172	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
173	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
174	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
175	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
176	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
177	  %  $$
178	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
179	  %  =
180	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
181	  %  $$"
182	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
183	  product).}
184	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
185	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
186	  \[
187	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
188	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
189	  \]
190	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
191	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
192	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
193	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
194	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
195	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
196	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
197	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
198	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
199	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
200	\end{definition}

202	\begin{definition}\leanok
203	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
204	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
206	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
207	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
208	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
209	  % L4220-L4228).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
212	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
213	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
214	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
215	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
216	  %  exactly $n$ times."
217	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
218	  powers).}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
220	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
221	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
222	  on \(m\):
223	  \[
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
225	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
226	    \qquad
227	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
228	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
229	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
230	  \]
231	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
232	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
233	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
234	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
235	  invertible.
236	\end{definition}

255	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}

329	\begin{definition}\leanok
330	[Right unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
331	  \label{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor}
333	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
334	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
335	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical right-unitor
336	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X
337	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
338	  \cref{def:unitModule}), the sheafification of the presheaf right unitor
339	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) composed with the counit
340	  isomorphism \cref{def:sheafificationCounitIso}.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{definition}\leanok
344	[Braiding of the sheaf tensor product]
345	  \label{def:tensorBraiding}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding}
347	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:schemeModuleSheafification,
348	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
349	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical
350	  braiding isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
351	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}\)
352	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), obtained by sheafifying
353	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) the symmetric braiding of the presheaf
354	  monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). It is the
355	  symmetry used in the inductive step of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
356	\end{definition}
357	
358	The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
359	below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
360	local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
361	coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
362	Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.
363	
364	\begin{definition}
365	
366	\leanok
367	[Grothendieck topology on the opens of a scheme]
368	  \label{def:snap_opensTopology}
369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensTopology}
370	  For a scheme \(X\) the \emph{opens topology} \(J\) is the canonical Grothendieck
371	  topology on the poset \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)\) of opens of the underlying
372	  topological space, in which a sieve covers an open \(U\) precisely when the opens
373	  it contains cover \(U\). It is the site on which the abelian sheafification, and
374	  the weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of local isomorphisms of abelian-group
375	  presheaves used in \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff} and
376	  \cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}, are taken.
377	\end{definition}
378	
379	\begin{lemma}\leanok
380	[Localization criterion for module sheafification]
381	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
382	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
383	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
384	    def:snap_opensTopology}
385	  For a morphism \(f : \mathcal{P} \to \mathcal{Q}\) of presheaves of
386	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the sheafification \((f)^{\#}\)
387	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules if
388	  and only if the underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves lies in the
389	  weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of the opens topology \(J\) on \(X\) (i.e.\ is a
390	  local isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves).
391	\end{lemma}
392	
393	\begin{proof}
394	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
395	  \leanok
396	  Module sheafification (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is the localization
397	  functor at the class \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying
398	  abelian-presheaf morphism lies in \(J.W\), i.e.\ the inverse image of \(J.W\) along
399	  the forgetful functor. The localization criterion for a reflective adjunction
400	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) then gives the stated
401	  equivalence.
402	\end{proof}
403	
404	\begin{lemma}\leanok
405	[The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism]
406	  \label{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
408	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
409	    def:snap_opensTopology}
410	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the underlying
411	  abelian-group presheaf morphism of the sheafification unit
412	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\)
413	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) is the abelian sheafification
414	  unit \(\mathcal{P} \to a(\mathcal{P})\); it therefore lies in the weak-equivalence
415	  class \(J.W\) of the opens topology.
416	\end{lemma}
417	
418	\begin{proof}
419	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
420	  \leanok
421	  The forgetful functor to abelian-group presheaves carries the module
422	  sheafification unit to the unit of the abelian associated-sheaf adjunction, which
423	  is a local isomorphism, hence a member of \(J.W\).
424	\end{proof}
425	
426	\begin{lemma}\leanok
427	[Sheafification inverts the localization unit]
428	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
429	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
430	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
431	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the sheafification
432	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})^{\#}\) of the localization unit is an isomorphism of sheaves
433	  of modules. This is the un-whiskered (\(m = 0\)) special case of
434	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}.
435	\end{lemma}
436	
437	\begin{proof}
438	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
439	  \leanok
440	  Feed the abelian local-isomorphism statement
441	  \cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit} through the localization criterion
442	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}.
443	\end{proof}
