445	\begin{definition}
446	
447	\leanok
448	[Abelian-carrier restriction map of a presheaf of modules]
449	  \label{def:snap_objRestrict}
450	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.objRestrict}
451	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) and a morphism
452	  \(f : U \to V\) in \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}}\) (an inclusion of opens
453	  \(V \subseteq U\)), the \emph{abelian-carrier restriction map}
454	  \[
455	    \mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(f) :
456	      \mathcal{P}(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{P}(V)
457	  \]
458	  is the underlying additive map of the presheaf restriction \(\mathcal{P}(f)\),
459	  viewed as a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear map between the carrier abelian groups. It is
460	  the form in which the presheaf restriction enters the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
461	  presheaves \cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf} and
462	  \cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}; its carriers are pinned to print as
463	  \(\mathcal{P}(U), \mathcal{P}(V)\) so that the tensor-of-restrictions maps unify
464	  on elementary tensors.
465	\end{definition}
466	
467	\begin{lemma}
468	
469	\leanok
470	[Abelian-carrier restriction on elements]
471	  \label{lem:snap_objRestrict_apply}
472	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.objRestrict_apply}
473	  \uses{def:snap_objRestrict}
474	  For a presheaf of modules \(\mathcal{P}\), a morphism \(f\) of opens, and a
475	  section \(x \in \mathcal{P}(U)\), one has
476	  \(\mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(f)\,(x) = \mathcal{P}(f)\,(x)\): the
477	  abelian-carrier restriction agrees with the presheaf restriction on elements, by
478	  definition.
479	\end{lemma}
480	
481	\begin{lemma}
482	
483	\leanok
484	[Identity law for the abelian-carrier restriction]
485	  \label{lem:snap_objRestrict_id}
486	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.objRestrict_id}
487	  \uses{def:snap_objRestrict}
488	  For a presheaf of modules \(\mathcal{P}\) and an open \(U\),
489	  \(\mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(\mathrm{id}_U) = \mathrm{id}\): restriction
490	  along the identity is the identity, since \(\mathcal{P}\) preserves identities.
491	\end{lemma}
492	
493	\begin{lemma}
494	
495	\leanok
496	[Composition law for the abelian-carrier restriction]
497	  \label{lem:snap_objRestrict_comp}
498	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.objRestrict_comp}
499	  \uses{def:snap_objRestrict}
500	  For a presheaf of modules \(\mathcal{P}\) and composable morphisms \(f : U \to V\),
501	  \(g : V \to W\) of opens, restriction along the composite is the composite of the
502	  restrictions,
503	  \[
504	    \mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(f \,;\, g)
505	      = \mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(g) \circ
506	        \mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(f),
507	  \]
508	  since \(\mathcal{P}\) preserves composition.
509	\end{lemma}
510	
511	\begin{definition}\leanok
512	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf]
513	  \label{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
514	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorDomainPresheaf}
515	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib, def:snap_objRestrict,
516	    lem:snap_objRestrict_id, lem:snap_objRestrict_comp}
517	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
518	  assignment \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\),
519	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the underlying restriction
520	  maps, is a functor \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is
521	  the codomain row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
522	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}); functoriality follows from the
523	  functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) (identity and composition laws
524	  by \(\otimes\)-induction).
525	\end{definition}
526	
527	\begin{definition}\leanok
528	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor triple presheaf]
529	  \label{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}
530	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorTriplePresheaf}
531	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib, def:snap_objRestrict,
532	    lem:snap_objRestrict_id, lem:snap_objRestrict_comp}
533	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
534	  assignment
535	  \[
536	    U \;\longmapsto\;
537	      \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
538	        \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
539	  \]
540	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the three underlying
541	  restriction maps --- those of \(\mathcal{P}\), of the structure presheaf
542	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the middle factor restricting along the ring restriction
543	  map), and of \(\mathcal{Q}\) --- is a functor
544	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is the \emph{domain}
545	  row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
546	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}): objectwise it is exactly the
547	  triple tensor \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
548	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) on which the two
549	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps of
550	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} act, its codomain row being
551	  the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf \cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}.
552	  Functoriality (the identity and composition laws of the restriction maps)
553	  follows from the functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) --- the
554	  \(\mathrm{map\_id}\) and \(\mathrm{map\_comp}\) laws --- checked on elementary
555	  tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
556	\end{definition}
557	
558	\begin{definition}\leanok
559	[Left-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
560	  \label{def:relTensorActL}
561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActL}
562	  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf,
563	    lem:snap_objRestrict_apply}
564	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
565	  \emph{left-action} natural transformation
566	  \[
567	    a_L : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
568	      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
569	    \;\longrightarrow\;
570	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
571	  \]
572	  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
573	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
574	  open \(U\), the additive map
575	  \[
576	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
577	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
578	    \longrightarrow
579	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
580	    \qquad
581	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto (s \cdot m) \otimes n,
582	  \]
583	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
584	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{P}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
585	  action map \(\mathrm{actLmap}\) of
586	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}; together with the parallel
587	  right-action transformation it is one of the two rows whose coequalizer
588	  presents the relative tensor presheaf \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
589	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}). Naturality in \(U\) is the
590	  compatibility of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction
591	  of \(s \cdot m\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), so \(a_L\) commutes with the
592	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps; this is checked on
593	  elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
594	\end{definition}
595	
596	\begin{definition}\leanok
597	[Right-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
598	  \label{def:relTensorActR}
599	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActR}
600	  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf,
601	    lem:snap_objRestrict_apply}
602	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
603	  \emph{right-action} natural transformation
604	  \[
605	    a_R : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
606	      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
607	    \;\longrightarrow\;
608	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
609	  \]
610	  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
611	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
612	  open \(U\), the additive map
613	  \[
614	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
615	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
616	    \longrightarrow
617	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
618	    \qquad
619	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n),
620	  \]
621	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
622	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{Q}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
623	  action map \(\mathrm{actRmap}\) of
624	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}, the mirror of the left-action
625	  transformation \(a_L\) (\cref{def:relTensorActL}); together \(a_L\) and \(a_R\)
626	  form the parallel pair whose coequalizer presents the relative tensor presheaf
627	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\). Naturality in \(U\) is the compatibility
628	  of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction of
629	  \(s \cdot n\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (n|_V)\), so \(a_R\) commutes with the
630	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps. The verification
631	  reduces to the \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-semilinearity of the restriction maps of
632	  \(\mathcal{Q}\), checked on elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
633	\end{definition}
634	
635	\begin{definition}
636	
637	\leanok
638	[Projection natural transformation of the coequalizer]
639	  \label{def:relTensorProj}
640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorProj}
641	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf, def:snap_objRestrict}
642	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
643	  \emph{projection} natural transformation
644	  \[
645	    \pi : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
646	      \;\longrightarrow\;
647	      \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
648	  \]
649	  from the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) to
650	  the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the presheaf-level monoidal tensor
651	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\) (Mathlib's
652	  \(\mathrm{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\)) is, on an open \(U\), the
653	  canonical quotient additive map
654	  \[
655	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
656	    \longrightarrow
657	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U),
658	    \qquad
659	    m \otimes n \longmapsto m \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} n,
660	  \]
661	  the objectwise projection \(\mathrm{projL}\) of
662	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}. It is the cofork of the
663	  relative-tensor coequalizer: the third leg, the cokernel of \(a_L - a_R\)
664	  (\cref{def:relTensorActL}, \cref{def:relTensorActR}). The apex is identified
665	  with the objectwise value of the presheaf monoidal product through
666	  \cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}.
667	
668	  \emph{Naturality.} The objectwise square asserts
669	  \[
670	    \pi_V \circ
671	      \bigl(\mathrm{restr}_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
672	        \mathrm{restr}_{\mathcal{Q}}\bigr)
673	    \;=\;
674	    \bigl((\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})(f)\bigr) \circ \pi_U
675	    \qquad (f : V \to U),
676	  \]
677	  both composites sending \(m \otimes n\) to
678	  \(\bigl(m|_V\bigr) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)} \bigl(n|_V\bigr)\). On elementary
679	  tensors the left-hand side reduces by functoriality of the
680	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of restriction maps followed by the defining formula of
681	  \(\mathrm{projL}\); the right-hand side reduces by \(\mathrm{projL}\) followed
682	  by the objectwise restriction formula for the presheaf monoidal product
683	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}, on elementary tensors:
684	  \((\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})(f)\,(m \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} n) =
685	  (m|_V) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)} (n|_V)\)). The two sides then agree by
686	  \(\otimes\)-induction.
687	
688	\end{definition}
689	
690	\begin{lemma}
691	
692	\leanok
693	[Cofork condition of the relative-tensor coequalizer]
694	  \label{lem:snap_relTensorActL_proj_eq}
695	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActL_proj_eq}
696	  \uses{def:relTensorActL, def:relTensorActR, def:relTensorProj,
697	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
698	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
699	  left- and right-action transformations compose equally with the projection,
700	  \[
701	    \pi \circ a_L \;=\; \pi \circ a_R,
702	  \]
703	  as natural transformations from the triple presheaf
704	  (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the underlying abelian-group presheaf of
705	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\), where \(a_L\)
706	  (\cref{def:relTensorActL}), \(a_R\) (\cref{def:relTensorActR}) are the two action
707	  rows and \(\pi\) (\cref{def:relTensorProj}) is the projection. This is the cofork
708	  (coequalizing) condition of the presheaf-level relative-tensor coequalizer
709	  presentation \cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}. It is checked
710	  componentwise: at each open \(U\) both sides agree by the objectwise coequalizing
711	  identity \(\pi_U \circ a_{L,U} = \pi_U \circ a_{R,U}\) of
712	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}.
713	\end{lemma}
714	
715	\begin{lemma}\leanok
716	[Right-whiskering preserves local isomorphisms of presheaves of modules]
717	  \label{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}
718	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.ztensor_whisker_localIso}
719	  \uses{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf,
720	    def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
721	    def:snap_opensTopology, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
722	  Let \(f : \mathcal{P} \to \mathcal{Q}\) be a morphism of presheaves of
723	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose underlying abelian-presheaf morphism is a
724	  local isomorphism (lies in the class \(J.W\) for the opens topology \(J\)
725	  on \(X\)). Then for any presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{R}\),
726	  the underlying abelian morphism of the right-whiskered map
727	  \(f \rhd \mathcal{R} : \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{R} \to
728	  \mathcal{Q} \otimes_p \mathcal{R}\) (in the presheaf monoidal structure of
729	  \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) again lies in \(J.W\).
730	\end{lemma}
731	
732	\begin{proof}
733	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf, def:relTensorTriplePresheaf,
734	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer, lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
735	    \leanok
736	  Two reductions.
737	
738	  \emph{(i) \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor whiskering preserves \(J.W\).} The category
739	  of abelian-group presheaves on \(X\) embeds, by a carrier-preserving
740	  equivalence, into presheaves valued in modules over a (universe lift of)
741	  \(\mathbb{Z}\). The latter value category is braided monoidal closed, so the
742	  pointwise (Day) monoidal structure on its presheaf category satisfies: right
743	  whiskering by a fixed object carries \(J.W\) into \(J.W\) (the reflection
744	  argument for monoidal sites — sheafification is a monoidal localization for a
745	  braided closed value category). An equivalence of value categories is a left
746	  adjoint in both directions, hence preserves sheafification both ways and
747	  identifies the two classes \(J.W\); transporting along it shows that the
748	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor whiskering \(g \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) of any
749	  \(g \in J.W\) by a fixed abelian presheaf again lies in \(J.W\).
750	
751	  \emph{(ii) Descent through the coequalizer presentation.} By
752	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}, the underlying abelian presheaf of
753	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{R}\) is the coequalizer (computed objectwise)
754	  of the two action rows out of the triple \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf
755	  (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}), and the underlying abelian morphism of
756	  \(f \rhd \mathcal{R}\) is the map induced on coequalizer points by
757	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-whiskering the two rows with \(f\). Abelian sheafification is
758	  a left adjoint, hence preserves these coequalizers; by (i) it inverts the
759	  whiskered rows, hence it inverts the induced map on coequalizer points. A
760	  morphism inverted by sheafification lies in \(J.W\), which is the claim.
761	\end{proof}
762	
763	\begin{lemma}[Abelian universal property of the relative tensor product]
764	  \label{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
765	  \lean{TensorProduct.liftAddHom}
766	  \mathlibok
767	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Basic}).}
768	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more
769	  generally, the abelian-group statement: \(M, N\) modules and \(P\) an additive
770	  commutative group). An additive-bilinear map
771	  \(f : M \to (N \to P)\) that is \emph{\(S\)-balanced}, i.e.\ satisfies
772	  \(f(s \cdot m)(n) = f(m)(s \cdot n)\) for all \(s \in S\), factors uniquely
773	  through the relative tensor product as an additive group homomorphism
774	  \(\widetilde{f} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with
775	  \(\widetilde{f}(m \otimes_S n) = f(m)(n)\). This is the abelian-group universal
776	  property of \(M \otimes_S N\): it is the quotient of \(M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N\)
777	  by the \(S\)-balancing relations.
778	\end{lemma}
779	
780	\begin{lemma}[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]
781	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
782	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
783	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
784	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
785	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
786	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
787	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
788	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
789	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
790	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
791	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
792	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
793	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
794	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
795	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
796	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
797	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
798	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac,
799	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap_tmul,
800	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap_tmul,
801	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_tmul,
802	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_apply,
803	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom_tmul}
804	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
805	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
806	  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
807	  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
808	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
809	  \[
810	    a_L,\, a_R :
811	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
812	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
813	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
814	  \]
815	  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
816	  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
817	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
818	  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
819	  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
820	\end{lemma}
821	
822	\begin{proof}  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
823	  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
824	  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
825	  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
826	  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
827	  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
828	  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
829	  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
830	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
831	  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
832	
833	  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
834	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
835	  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
836	  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
837	  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
838	  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).
839	
840	  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
841	  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
842	  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
843	  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
844	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
845	  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
846	  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
847	  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
848	  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
849	  colimit cofork.
850	
851	\end{proof}
852	
853	\begin{lemma}\leanok
854	[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
855	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
856	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
857	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
858	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
859	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
860	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib,
861	    def:relTensorActL, def:relTensorActR, def:relTensorProj}
862	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
863	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
864	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
865	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
866	  parallel pair
867	  \[
868	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
869	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
870	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
871	  \]
872	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
873	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
874	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
875	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
876	\end{lemma}
877	
878	\begin{proof}
879	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
880	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
881	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
882	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib,
883	    def:relTensorActL, def:relTensorActR, def:relTensorProj,
884	    lem:snap_relTensorActL_proj_eq}
885	    \leanok
886	  The argument promotes the objectwise coequalizer of
887	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} to the functor category, in
888	  three steps.
889	
890	  \textbf{1. Objectwise.} For each open \(U\), apply
891	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} with \(S = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
892	  \(M = \mathcal{P}(U)\), \(N = \mathcal{Q}(U)\): the abelian group
893	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is the coequalizer
894	  in \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps
895	  \[
896	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
897	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
898	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
899	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
900	  \]
901	  with cofork map the projection \(\pi_U\).
902	
903	  \textbf{2. Naturality / promotion.} The three objectwise families are precisely
904	  the natural transformations of presheaves on \(X\)
905	  \[
906	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
907	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
908	    \;\overset{a_L,\,a_R}{\rightrightarrows}\;
909	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
910	    \;\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\;
911	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
912	  \]
913	  built in the previous definitions: the parallel pair is the left- and
914	  right-action transformations \(a_L\) (\cref{def:relTensorActL}) and \(a_R\)
915	  (\cref{def:relTensorActR}) from the triple presheaf
916	  (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf
917	  (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}), and the cofork leg is the projection
918	  \(\pi\) (\cref{def:relTensorProj}) onto the apex. These are morphisms of
919	  functors \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\); naturality of
920	  each in \(U\) is the compatibility of the module action with the restriction
921	  maps of \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\) (the restriction of
922	  \(s \cdot m\) is \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), and likewise for the projection),
923	  recorded in those definitions. Objectwise at each \(U\) the cofork
924	  \((a_L, a_R, \pi)_U\) is the colimit cofork of step 1. Colimits in a functor
925	  category are computed objectwise --- by
926	  \cref{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib} a cocone is a colimit as
927	  soon as every evaluation \((\mathrm{ev}_U)\) of it is --- so the objectwise
928	  coequalizers of step 1 promote to a coequalizer of the displayed parallel pair
929	  \(a_L, a_R\) with cofork \(\pi\) in
930	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\).
931	
932	  \textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
933	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
934	  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
935	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
936	  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
937	  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
938	  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
939	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
940	  along this identification exhibits
941	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
942	  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\).
943	\end{proof}
944	
945	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise colimits in a functor category]
946	  \label{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib}
947	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits}
948	  \mathlibok
949	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
950	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FunctorCategory.Basic}).}
951	  Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a category and \(\mathcal{A}\) a category in which the
952	  relevant colimits exist. A cocone over a diagram
953	  \(F : \mathcal{J} \to (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A})\) in the functor category is
954	  a colimit cocone as soon as, for every object \(U\) of \(\mathcal{D}\), its image
955	  under evaluation \(\mathrm{ev}_U : (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A}) \to \mathcal{A}\)
956	  is a colimit cocone in \(\mathcal{A}\). Equivalently, colimits in a functor
957	  category are computed objectwise.
958	\end{lemma}
959	
960	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise value of the presheaf tensor product]
961	  \label{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
962	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_obj}
963	  \mathlibok
964	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
965	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
966	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
967	  commutative rings and \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\) presheaves of modules over
968	  \(R\). For every object \(U\) the value of their monoidal product
969	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is the objectwise relative tensor
970	  product of modules,
971	  \[
972	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
973	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
974	  \]
975	  with the restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps.
976	\end{lemma}
977	
978	\begin{lemma}\leanok
979	[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
980	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
981	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
982	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
983	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
984	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
985	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
986	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
987	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
988	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
989	  the sheafification adjunction
990	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
991	  presheaves of modules, and write
992	  \[
993	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
994	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
995	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
996	  \]
997	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
998	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
999	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
1000	  \[
1001	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
1002	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
1003	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1004	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
1005	  \]
1006	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
1007	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
1008	\end{lemma}
1009	
1010	\begin{proof}
1011	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1012	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
1013	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
1014	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
1015	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
1016	    lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
1017	    \leanok
1018	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
1019	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
1020	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
1021	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
1022	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
1023	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
1024	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
1025	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
1026	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
1027	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
1028	
1029	  \textbf{The unit is a local isomorphism.} The sheafification unit
1030	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) has underlying
1031	  abelian-presheaf morphism in \(J.W\)
1032	  (\cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit} — the defining property of the
1033	  associated-sheaf unit for the topology \(J\)).
1034	
1035	  \textbf{Whiskering preserves \(J.W\).} By
1036	  \cref{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}, right-whiskering a morphism of
1037	  presheaves of modules whose underlying abelian morphism lies in \(J.W\) by
1038	  the fixed presheaf \(\mathcal{Q}\) yields a morphism whose underlying
1039	  abelian morphism again lies in \(J.W\) (proved there by descending the
1040	  pointwise monoidal-whiskering statement through the relative-tensor
1041	  coequalizer presentation of
1042	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}). Applied to
1043	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}}\), the underlying abelian morphism of
1044	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\), so
1045	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
1046	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
1047	\end{proof}
1048	
1049	\begin{corollary}\leanok
1050	[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
1051	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
1052	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}

1053	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1054	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1055	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1056	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
1057	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
1058	  \[
1059	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
1060	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
1061	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
1062	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1063	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1064	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
1065	  \]
1066	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
1067	  arguments.
1068	\end{corollary}
1069	
1070	\begin{proof}
1071	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1072	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1073	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1074	    \leanok
1075	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
1076	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
1077	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
1078	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
1079	  \[
1080	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
1081	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
1082	    \qquad
1083	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
1084	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
1085	  \]
1086	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
1087	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
1088	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
1089	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
1090	  \[
1091	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
1092	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
1093	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1094	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
1095	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
1096	  \]
1097	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
1098	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
1099	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
1100	  morphism
1101	  \[
1102	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
1103	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
1104	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1105	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
1106	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
1107	  \]
1108	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
1109	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
1110	  \[
1111	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
1112	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
1113	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1114	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
1115	  \]
1116	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
1117	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
1118	  composite
1119	  \[
1120	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
1121	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
1122	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
1123	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
1124	  \]
1125	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1126	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
1127	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
1128	\end{proof}

1130	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1131	[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
1132	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1133	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
1134	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
1135	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
1136	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
1137	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
1138	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1139	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
1140	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1141	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
1142	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
1143	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
1144	  %  (formulation omitted)."
1145	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
1146	  power isomorphisms).}
1147	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
1148	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1149	  \[
1150	    \mu_{m,m'} :
1151	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1152	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
1153	  \]
1154	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
1155	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
1156	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
1157	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1158	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
1159	\end{lemma}
1160	
1161	\begin{proof}
1162	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
1163	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
1164	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1165	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1166	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1167	    \leanok
1168	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
1169	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
1170	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
1171	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
1172	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
1173	
1174	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
1175	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
1176	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
1177	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
1178	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
1179	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
1180	  sheaf tensor products
1181	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
1182	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
1183	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
1184	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
1185	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
1186	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
1187	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
1188	  of the tensor product is never used.
1189	
1190	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
1191	  \(m = 0\),
1192	  \[
1193	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1194	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1195	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1196	  \]
1197	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
1198	  recursive definition
1199	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
1200	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
1201	  \[
1202	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1203	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
1204	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1205	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1206	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
1207	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1208	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
1209	  \]
1210	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
1211	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
1212	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
1213	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
1214	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
1215	  \[
1216	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
1217	      \mathcal{L}
1218	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
1219	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
1220	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
1221	  \]
1222	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
1223	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
1224	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
1225	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1226	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
1227	
1228	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
1229	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
1230	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
1231	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
1232	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
1233	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
1234	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
1235	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
1236	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1237	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
1238	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
1239	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
1240	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
1241	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
1242	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
1243	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
1244	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
1245	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
1246	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
1247	\end{proof}

1249	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
1250	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
1251	
1252	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
1253	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
1254	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
1255	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
1256	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
1257	global sections.
1258	
1259	\begin{definition}\leanok
1260	[Section multiplication]
1261	  \label{def:sectionMul}
1262	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
1263	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1264	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1265	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
1266	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
1267	  \[
1268	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
1269	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
1270	    \;\longrightarrow\;
1271	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
1272	  \]
1273	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
1274	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
1275	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
1276	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
1277	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
1278	  the sheafification unit
1279	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
1280	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
1281	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
1282	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
1283	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
1284	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
1285	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
1286	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
1287	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
1288	\end{definition}
1289	
1290	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
1291	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
1292	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
1293	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1294	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
1295	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
1296	  the two composites
1297	  \[
1298	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
1299	    \longrightarrow
1300	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
1301	    \cong
1302	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
1303	  \]
1304	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
1305	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
1306	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
1307	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
1308	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
1309	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
1310	\end{lemma}
1311	
1312	\begin{proof}
1313	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1314	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1315	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
1316	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
1317	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
1318	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
1319	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
1320	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
1321	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
1322	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
1323	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
1324	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
1325	  section multiplications.
1326	\end{proof}
1327	
1328	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
1329	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
1330	
1331	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
1332	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
1333	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
1334	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
1335	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
1336	
1337	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
1338	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1339	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
1340	  \mathlibok
1341	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
1342	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1343	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
1344	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
1345	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
1346	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
1347	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
1348	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
1349	  \(1 \in A_0\).
1350	\end{lemma}

1379	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
1380	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the

1385	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
1386	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
1387	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
1388	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
1389	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
1390	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
1391	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
1392	  multiplication is the composite
1393	  \[
1394	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
1395	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
1396	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
1397	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
1398	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
1399	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
1400	  \]
1401	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
1402	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
1403	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
1404	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
1405	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
1406	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
1407	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
1408	\end{lemma}

1410	\begin{proof}
1411	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
1412	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1413	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
1414	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
1415	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
1416	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
1417	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
1418	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
1419	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
1420	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
1421	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1422	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
1423	  commutative-semiring structure.
1424	\end{proof}

1446	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on

1448	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)

1475	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication

1477	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of

959	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}), so its Cramer inverse is again the

982	\begin{lemma}\leanok
983	[Cramer-inverse cocycle on the triple overlap]
984	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}
985	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle_matrix}
986	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
987	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
988	    lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_cocycle}
989	  Over the common triple-overlap ring (the localisation carrying the three charts'
990	  data to \(V_{IJK}\)) the Cramer inverses of the localised minors satisfy the
991	  multiplicative cocycle identity
992	  \[
993	    (X^J_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}.
994	  \]
995	  This is the pure matrix-algebra core of (C2), independent of any sheaf data.
996	\end{lemma}
997	\begin{proof}
998	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
999	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
1000	    lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_cocycle}
1001	  Write all matrices over the common triple-overlap ring. The image matrix of the
1002	  transition \(\theta_{I,J}\) is \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\), and its \(K\)-minor is
1003	  computed by restricting to the \(K\)-indexed columns; since taking a column
1004	  submatrix commutes with left multiplication (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}),
1005	  \[
1006	    X^J_K \;=\; \bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr)_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_K .
1007	  \]
1008	  Both \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\) have unit determinant
1009	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so this product of invertibles inverts
1010	  by \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\):
1011	  \[
1012	    (X^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J).
1013	  \]
1014	  Multiplying on the right by \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) and cancelling \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1\)
1015	  via the two-sided Cramer identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}, the
1016	  cancellation packaged in \cref{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}) gives
1017	  \((X^J_K)^{-1} (X^I_J)^{-1} = (X^I_K)^{-1}\). The agreement of the three image
1018	  matrices over the triple overlap that licenses these manipulations is the content of
1019	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}, the matrix form of the scheme-level cocycle
1020	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}.
1021	\end{proof}

1023	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1024	[Scalar endomorphism is natural under pullback]
1025	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}
1026	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_pullback}
1027	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1028	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(a \in \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) a
1029	  global function, with comorphism on global sections
1030	  \(p^{\sharp} : \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\).
1031	  Write \(q : p^{*}\mathbf{1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathbf{1}\) for the unit-pullback comparison
1032	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index). Then the pullback of the scalar endomorphism
1033	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\) is conjugate, through \(q\), to the scalar endomorphism of the
1034	  base-changed function:
1035	  \[
1036	    p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\bigr)
1037	      \;=\; q^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(p^{\sharp} a\bigr) \,\circ\, q
1038	  \]
1039	  as endomorphisms of \(p^{*}\mathbf{1}\); equivalently
1040	  \(q \circ p^{*}(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(p^{\sharp} a) \circ q\). This is the
1041	  single-entry naturality atom underlying the matrix-level statement
1042	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}.
1043	\end{lemma}
1044	\begin{proof}
1045	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1046	  The scalar endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is multiplication
1047	  by the global section \(a\) on the unit module \(\mathbf 1 = \mathcal{O}_S\). Pulling back
1048	  along \(p\) sends multiplication by \(a\) to multiplication by its image, and the comorphism
1049	  \(p^{\sharp}\) is precisely the action of pullback on global functions, so
1050	  \(p^{*}(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a))\) is multiplication by \(p^{\sharp}a\) on \(p^{*}\mathbf 1\). The
1051	  comparison \(q\) is an isomorphism of unit modules intertwining this multiplication on
1052	  \(p^{*}\mathbf 1\) with multiplication by \(p^{\sharp}a\) on \(\mathbf 1\); conjugating back
1053	  along \(q\) therefore yields the claimed identity. The verification is a single computation on
1054	  the unit module, where multiplication by a function commutes with the natural comparison
1055	  \(q\).
1056	\end{proof}

1058	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1059	[Matrix endomorphism is natural under pullback]
1060	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}
1061	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_pullback}
1062	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1063	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
1064	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), and
1065	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\). Let
1066	  \(p^{\sharp}M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T))\) be the entrywise
1067	  image of \(M\) under the comorphism \(p^{\sharp}\), and let
1068	  \(Q : p^{*}(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) be the free-pullback
1069	  comparison \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at the rank-\(d\) index set. Then the
1070	  pullback of the matrix endomorphism \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is conjugate, through \(Q\), to
1071	  the matrix endomorphism of the base-changed matrix:
1072	  \[
1073	    p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\bigr)
1074	      \;=\; Q^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(p^{\sharp}M) \,\circ\, Q .
1075	  \]
1076	\end{lemma}
1077	\begin{proof}
1078	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1079	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
1080	  By \cref{def:gr_matrixEnd}, \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is the biproduct-assembled endomorphism
1081	  of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} = \bigoplus_{k} \mathbf 1\) whose \((k,\ell)\)-component is
1082	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{k,\ell})\). The pullback functor \(p^{*}\) is a left adjoint, hence
1083	  additive and biproduct-preserving; through the coproduct comparison underlying the
1084	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} it carries the biproduct presentation
1085	  of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) to that of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) and the assembled morphism to the
1086	  morphism reassembled from the pulled-back components, the reassociation of the iterated
1087	  pullbacks being \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. On each single \((k,\ell)\)-component the
1088	  pulled-back scalar endomorphism is, by \cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}, the
1089	  comparison-conjugate of \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(p^{\sharp}M_{k,\ell}) =
1090	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}((p^{\sharp}M)_{k,\ell})\). Reassembling over the biproduct, the conjugating
1091	  one-element comparisons combine into the single free comparison \(Q\), giving
1092	  \(p^{*}(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)) = Q^{-1}\circ\mathrm{matrixEnd}(p^{\sharp}M)\circ Q\).
1093	\end{proof}

1345	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

1353	  \[

1371	section-level descent \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}; its one remaining content is the
1372	\emph{overlap-compatibility} of the family \((u^I)\) with the bundle gluing
1373	\((g_{I,J})\). The next four blocks isolate that compatibility: a rectangular analogue of
1374	the square matrix-endomorphism API (\cref{def:gr_matrixEnd},
1375	\cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}) for the
1376	\(r \to d\) chart quotient (\cref{def:gr_matrixEndRect},
1377	\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}), the
1378	adjunction transposition that turns the descent condition into a pullback-level identity
1379	(\cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose}), and the matrix-core assembly
1380	that closes it (\cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotient_overlap}).

1409	\begin{lemma}

1412	[Rectangular matrix homomorphism is natural under pullback]
1413	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}
1414	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_pullback}
1415	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback,

1417	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and
1418	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), with entrywise
1419	  base-changed matrix \(p^{\sharp}M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(T,\mathcal{O}_T))\).
1420	  Write \(Q_r : p^{*}\mathcal{O}_S^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) and
1421	  \(Q_d : p^{*}\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) for the
1422	  free-pullback comparisons (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) at the index sets
1423	  \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Then the pullback of
1424	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) is conjugate, through \(Q_r\) and \(Q_d\), to the
1425	  rectangular homomorphism of the base-changed matrix:
1426	  \[

1428	      \;=\; Q_d^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(p^{\sharp}M) \,\circ\, Q_r .
1429	  \]

1431	\begin{proof}
1432	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1433	    def:modules_pullbackComp, lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}
1434	  Identical to the square case \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}, with the source and target
1435	  biproducts now indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\) respectively. The
1436	  pullback functor \(p^{*}\), a left adjoint, is additive and biproduct-preserving; through
1437	  the coproduct comparisons underlying the free-pullback isomorphisms
1438	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} it carries the biproduct presentation of
1439	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,r}\) (resp. \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\)) to that of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)
1440	  (resp. \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\)), the reassociation of the iterated pullbacks being
1441	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. On each single \((k,\ell)\)-component the pulled-back
1442	  scalar endomorphism is, by \cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}, the comparison-conjugate of
1443	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}((p^{\sharp}M)_{k,\ell})\). Reassembling over the two biproducts, the
1444	  conjugating one-element comparisons combine into \(Q_r\) on the source and \(Q_d\) on the
1445	  target, giving \(p^{*}(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)) = Q_d^{-1}\circ
1446	  \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(p^{\sharp}M)\circ Q_r\).
1447	\end{proof}

1449	\begin{lemma}
1450	
1451	\leanok
1452	[Square-after-rectangular composition law]
1453	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}
1454	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_comp}
1455	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add,
1456	    lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
1457	  For \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\) and
1458	  \(N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), post-composing the
1459	  rectangular homomorphism with a square one realises the matrix product, with the order
1460	  reversed by the contravariance of the component indexing exactly as in
1461	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}:
1462	  \[
1463	    \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)
1464	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(N \cdot M),
1465	  \]
1466	  the composite \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)}
1467	  \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) (here
1468	  \(N \cdot M\) is the \(d \times r\) product).
1469	\end{lemma}
1470	\begin{proof}
1471	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add,
1472	    lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
1473	  The same biproduct-extensionality computation as \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}, the only
1474	  change being that the leftmost (input) biproduct is indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) rather
1475	  than \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). The composite of two biproduct-assembled morphisms is again
1476	  biproduct-assembled, with \((k,\ell)\)-component the sum over the middle index \(m\) of the
1477	  composites of components, \(\sum_m \mathrm{scalarEnd}(N_{k,m}) \circ
1478	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{m,\ell})\). Each composite of scalar endomorphisms is the scalar
1479	  endomorphism of the product (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}) and a finite sum of scalar
1480	  endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}); hence
1481	  that component is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(\sum_m N_{k,m} M_{m,\ell}) =
1482	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}((N\cdot M)_{k,\ell})\), the \((k,\ell)\)-component of
1483	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(N\cdot M)\).
1484	\end{proof}

1542	  the pullback-level identity \(g_{I,J} \circ f_{IJ}^{*} u^I = (t_{IJ}\circ f_{JI})^{*} u^J\)

1545	\begin{proof}

1557	  base-change naturality \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback} identifies \(f_{IJ}^{*} u^I\),
1558	  through the free-pullback comparisons, with \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\) of the localised

1576	\begin{definition}
1577	
1578	\leanok
1579	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
1580	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
1581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
1582	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
1583	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
1584	    def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
1585	    def:gr_modules_glueHom, lem:gr_tautologicalQuotient_overlap}
1586	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1587	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1588	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
1589	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1590	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
1591	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
1592	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
1593	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1594	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
1595	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
1596	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
1597	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
1598	  \[
1599	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
1600	  \]
1601	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
1602	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). At the
1603	  module level this overlap compatibility -- the equalising condition consumed by the
1604	  section-level descent \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom} -- is exactly
1605	  \cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotient_overlap}, whose proof routes the matrix identity above
1606	  through the rectangular pullback/composition API
1607	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}) and the
1608	  adjunction transpose \cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose}.
1609	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
1610	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
1611	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
1612	  \[
1613	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
1614	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
1615	  \]
1616	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
1617	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
1618	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
1619	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
1620	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
1621	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
1622	\end{definition}
