1624	\section{The functor of points}
1625	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
1626	
1627	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
1628	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
1629	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
1630	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
1631	identities that make that action functorial.
1632	
1633	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
1634	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
1635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
1636	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
1637	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
1638	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
1639	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
1640	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
1641	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
1642	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
1643	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1644	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1645	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
1646	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
1647	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
1648	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
1649	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
1650	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
1651	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
1652	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
1653	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
1654	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
1655	  functor's value at \(T\).
1656	
1657	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
1658	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
1659	  \[
1660	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
1661	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
1662	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1663	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
1664	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
1665	  \]
1666	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
1667	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
1668	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
1669	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
1670	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
1671	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
1672	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
1673	  map of equivalence classes.
1674	\end{definition}
1675	
1676	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
1677	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
1678	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.
1679	
1680	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1681	[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1682	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1683	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1684	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1685	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
1686	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
1687	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
1688	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
1689	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1690	\end{lemma}
1691	
1692	\begin{proof}
1693	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1694	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
1695	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
1696	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
1697	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
1698	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
1699	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
1700	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
1701	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
1702	\end{proof}
1703	
1704	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1705	[Identity coherence at the free sheaf]
1706	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}
1707	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_id}
1708	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1709	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\) and an index set \(I\), the free-pullback
1710	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1) \cong \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) coincides
1711	  with the component at the free sheaf \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) of the pullback-identity
1712	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1713	\end{lemma}
1714	
1715	\begin{proof}
1716	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1717	  Both sides are cocones on the coproduct \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\); by coproduct
1718	  extensionality it suffices to check equality after precomposing with each coprojection
1719	  \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element case
1720	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}.
1721	\end{proof}
1722	
1723	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1724	[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1725	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1726	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1727	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1728	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
1729	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
1730	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
1731	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
1732	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
1733	\end{lemma}
1734	
1735	\begin{proof}
1736	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp,
1737	    lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1738	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
1739	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
1740	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
1741	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The compatibility of the mate
1742	  with the adjunction hom-equivalences is supplied by
1743	  \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}, the reusable conjugate-naturality engine. The
1744	  pentagon coherence of the iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation
1745	  well-defined.
1746	\end{proof}
1747	
1748	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1749	[Mate/conjugate compatibility of the adjunction hom-equivalence]
1750	  \label{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1751	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1752	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2\) be two adjunctions and \(\alpha\) a natural
1753	  transformation between their left adjoints. For a morphism \(f\) the hom-equivalence of
1754	  \(\mathrm{adj}_2\) applied to \(\alpha_c \circ f\) equals the hom-equivalence of
1755	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1\) applied to \(f\), post-composed with the component at \(d\) of the
1756	  conjugate (mate) of \(\alpha\). This is the general naturality of the conjugate
1757	  construction against the two hom-equivalences; it is the reusable engine behind
1758	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
1759	\end{lemma}
1760	
1761	\begin{proof}
1762	
1763	  Both sides unfold to the same pasting of unit/counit triangles for the two adjunctions;
1764	  the equality is the standard compatibility of the mate of \(\alpha\) with the
1765	  hom-set bijections.
1766	\end{proof}
1767	
1768	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1769	[Composite coherence at the free sheaf]
1770	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1771	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1772	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1773	  The composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}: for composable \(a, b\) and an
1774	  index set \(I\), the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b \circ a\) at \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\)
1775	  factors through \(a^{*}\) of the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b\), the one for \(a\), and
1776	  the iterated-pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}.
1777	\end{lemma}
1778	
1779	\begin{proof}
1780	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1781	  By coproduct extensionality it suffices to check the identity after precomposing with each
1782	  coprojection \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element
1783	  composite coherence \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} (with the base-ring sheaf
1784	  tracked through each pullback).
1785	\end{proof}
1786	
1787	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1788	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
1789	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
1790	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
1791	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
1792	functoriality laws (identity and composition) of the Grassmannian
1793	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.
1794	
1795	\begin{definition}
1796	
1797	\leanok
1798	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
1799	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
1800	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
1801	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
1802	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1803	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1804	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1805	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
1806	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1807	  % "represents the
1808	  %  contravariant functor
1809	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1810	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1811	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1812	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1813	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
1814	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1815	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
1816	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
1817	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
1818	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
1819	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
1820	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
1821	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
1822	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1823	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
1824	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
1825	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
1826	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
1827	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
1828	  \[
1829	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
1830	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
1831	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
1832	  \]
1833	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
1834	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
1835	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
1836	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
1837	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
1838	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
1839	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
1840	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
1841	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
1842	\end{definition}
1843

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68

77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
8	
9	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
10	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
11	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
12	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
13	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
14	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
15	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
16	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
17	single non-zero element of the base ring.
18	
19	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
20	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
21	\(\F\).
22	
23	\section{Generic flatness}
24	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
25	
26	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
27	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
28	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
29	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
30	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
31	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
32	non-zero element of the base ring.
33	
34	\begin{theorem}
35	
36	
37	
38	\leanok
39	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
40	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
42	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
43	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
44	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
45	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
48	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
49	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
50	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."

4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).

2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}

5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).

1844	\section{The universal property}
1845	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
1846	
1847	The representability theorem \cref{thm:grassmannian_universal_property} has two halves:
1848	the forward map \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u\rangle\)
1849	and its inverse, which manufactures a morphism \(T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) out of a rank-\(d\)
1850	quotient \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\). The forward map needs the tautological
1851	quotient to actually be a quotient -- that \(u\) is an epimorphism
1852	(\cref{lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi}). The inverse is built Zariski-locally and glued: for
1853	each size-\(d\) subset \(I\) one isolates the open locus \(T_I \subseteq T\) where the
1854	\(I\)-indexed columns of \(q\) form a basis of \(\mathcal{F}\), classifies \(T_I \to U^I\)
1855	there, and glues over the cover \(\{T_I\}\). The blocks of this section supply the four
1856	ingredients of that inverse: the iso-locus of a sheaf morphism (\cref{def:isoLocus}) and
1857	the fact that a morphism becomes invertible after restriction to its iso-locus
1858	(\cref{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}); the chart locus \(T_I\) (\cref{def:chartLocus})
1859	and the proof that the chart loci cover \(T\) (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}); and the
1860	glued classifying morphism itself (\cref{def:grPointOfRankQuotient}).
1861	
1862	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1863	[The tautological quotient is an epimorphism]
1864	  \label{lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi}
1865	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient_epi}
1866	  \uses{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi, def:tautological_quotient}
1867	  The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
1868	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}) is an epimorphism of
1869	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-modules.
1870	\end{lemma}
1871	\begin{proof}
1872	  \uses{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi, def:tautological_quotient}
1873	  Being an epimorphism of sheaves of modules is local on the base: a morphism is epi
1874	  precisely when its restriction to every member of an open cover is epi, since the
1875	  cokernel is computed chartwise and a sheaf vanishing on a cover vanishes. The charts
1876	  \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and over \(U^I\) the tautological quotient,
1877	  transported through the trivialisation \(\mathcal{U}|_{U^I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
1878	  of \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}, is the chart quotient \(u^I :
1879	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}).
1880	  Each \(u^I\) is a split epimorphism: its \(I\)-minor is the identity \(1_{d\times d}\),
1881	  so the coordinate inclusion \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow
1882	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) on the \(I\)-columns is a section of \(u^I\), whence \(u^I\) is
1883	  epi (\cref{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}). As \(u^I\) is epi on every chart, \(u\) is epi.
1884	\end{proof}

1886	\begin{definition}\leanok
1887	[Iso-locus of a morphism of sheaves of modules]
1888	  \label{def:isoLocus}
1889	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isoLocus}
1890	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) a morphism of sheaves
1891	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The \emph{iso-locus} \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi) \subseteq X\)
1892	  is the open subscheme
1893	  \[
1894	    \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)
1895	      \;=\; \bigsqcup\,\bigl\{\, U \subseteq X \text{ open} \;:\;
1896	        \varphi|_U := (\iota_U^{*}\varphi) \text{ is an isomorphism} \,\bigr\},
1897	  \]
1898	  the supremum (union) of all opens \(U\) over which the pullback \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) of
1899	  \(\varphi\) to the open subscheme \(U\) is an isomorphism of sheaves of
1900	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules. Equivalently, a point \(t \in X\) lies in
1901	  \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) iff \(\varphi\) restricts to an isomorphism over some open
1902	  neighbourhood of \(t\). It is the largest open on which \(\varphi\) is invertible; Mathlib
1903	  has no iso-locus primitive for morphisms of sheaves of modules, so it is set up directly
1904	  as this supremum.
1905	\end{definition}
1906	
1907	\begin{lemma}[Stalk-wise iso criterion]
1908	  \label{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
1909	  \lean{TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso}
1910	  \mathlibok
1911	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1912	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves on a topological space \(X\) valued in a
1913	  concrete category whose forgetful functor reflects isomorphisms and preserves limits and
1914	  filtered colimits, and let \(f : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}\) be a morphism. If the
1915	  induced map on stalks \(f_t : \mathcal{F}_t \to \mathcal{G}_t\) is an isomorphism for every
1916	  point \(t \in X\), then \(f\) is an isomorphism.
1917	\end{lemma}
1918	
1919	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1920	[A morphism is invertible over its iso-locus]
1921	  \label{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
1922	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
1923	  \uses{def:isoLocus, lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
1924	  For a morphism \(\varphi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of
1925	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the pullback of \(\varphi\) to the iso-locus
1926	  (\cref{def:isoLocus}),
1927	  \[
1928	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\varphi
1929	      \;:\;
1930	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\mathcal{M}
1931	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1932	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\mathcal{N},
1933	  \]
1934	  is an isomorphism.
1935	\end{lemma}
1936	\begin{proof}
1937	  \uses{def:isoLocus, lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
1938	  Being an isomorphism is a stalk-local property: by the stalk-wise criterion
1939	  (\cref{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}) it suffices to show that the pulled-back
1940	  morphism induces an isomorphism on the stalk at every point \(t \in
1941	  \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\). By the definition of the iso-locus as a union of opens on
1942	  which \(\varphi\) restricts to an isomorphism (\cref{def:isoLocus}), each such \(t\) has an
1943	  open neighbourhood \(U \subseteq \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) with \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) an
1944	  isomorphism; restriction of an isomorphism is an isomorphism, and pullback commutes with
1945	  taking stalks, so the stalk of the morphism at \(t\) coincides with the stalk of
1946	  \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) at \(t\), which is invertible. Hence every stalk map is an
1947	  isomorphism, and the criterion concludes that the global pullback over
1948	  \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism.
1949	\end{proof}

1951	\begin{definition}\leanok
1952	[Chart locus of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
1953	  \label{def:chartLocus}
1954	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartLocus}
1955	  \uses{def:isoLocus, def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_rankQuotient}
1956	  Let \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) be a rank-\(d\) quotient of
1957	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) on a scheme \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}) and \(I \subseteq
1958	  \{1,\dots,r\}\) a size-\(d\) subset. The \emph{chart composite} is the morphism of sheaves
1959	  of modules
1960	  \[
1961	    s_I \circ q
1962	      \;:\;
1963	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1964	      \overset{\ s_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1965	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1966	  \]
1967	  obtained by precomposing the quotient \(q\) with the inclusion \(s_I\) of the
1968	  \(I\)-indexed coordinates of the trivial bundle. The \emph{chart locus}
1969	  \(T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I) \subseteq T\) is the iso-locus
1970	  (\cref{def:isoLocus}) of this chart composite:
1971	  \[
1972	    T_I \;=\; \mathrm{isoLocus}(s_I \circ q),
1973	  \]
1974	  the largest open of \(T\) over which the \(I\)-columns of \(q\) form a basis of
1975	  \(\mathcal{F}\) -- i.e.\ over which \(s_I \circ q\) is an isomorphism. Over \(T_I\) the
1976	  quotient \(q\) is therefore presented, after trivialising \(\mathcal{F}\) by
1977	  \((s_I \circ q)^{-1}\), by a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-minor is the identity,
1978	  exactly the shape of the universal matrix \(X^I\) realising the chart quotient \(u^I\)
1979	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
1980	\end{definition}

1982	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1983	[The chart loci cover \(T\)]
1984	  \label{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}
1985	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartLocus_isOpenCover}
1986	  \uses{def:chartLocus, def:gr_rankQuotient, lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
1987	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1988	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
1989	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L823-L828).
1990	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1991	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
1992	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
1993	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
1994	  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
1995	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
1996	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
1997	  %  on $U^I_J$."
1998	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1999	  For a rank-\(d\) quotient \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)
2000	  on \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}), the family of chart loci
2001	  \(\{\,T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I)\,\}_{\#(I) = d}\) (\cref{def:chartLocus}) is an open
2002	  cover of \(T\): \(\bigcup_{\#(I)=d} T_I = T\).
2003	\end{lemma}
2004	\begin{proof}
2005	  \uses{def:chartLocus, def:gr_rankQuotient}
2006	  % NOTE (review iter-078): the FORMALIZED proof diverges from this prose. The Lean
2007	  % (chartLocus_isOpenCover) does NOT use the stalkwise fibre-map / Nakayama propagation
2008	  % below; it splits the epi of free sheaves GLOBALLY over an affine W via projectivity
2009	  % (exists_section_of_epi_free_spec / exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect), then
2010	  % evaluates the right inverse at the residue field (exists_isUnit_submatrix: a
2011	  % right-invertible matrix over a field has an invertible d-column minor), and cuts out
2012	  % the basic open from the minor determinant. Endpoints coincide (minor invertible on a
2013	  % basic open) but the mechanism differs — a blueprint-writer should rewrite this proof
2014	  % block to name the affine-splitting route so it can guide future provers.
2015	  Each \(T_I\) is open, being an iso-locus (\cref{def:isoLocus}). It remains to show the
2016	  \(T_I\) cover \(T\), i.e.\ that every point \(t \in T\) lies in some \(T_I\). Fix \(t\) and
2017	  work in an open neighbourhood \(V \ni t\) trivialising \(\mathcal{F}\),
2018	  \(\mathcal{F}|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V^{\,d}\) (such \(V\) exists because \(\mathcal{F}\) is
2019	  locally free of rank \(d\)); over \(V\) the quotient \(q\) is presented by a \(d \times r\)
2020	  matrix \(M\) of sections of \(\mathcal{O}_V\). Because \(q\) is an epimorphism
2021	  (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}), its reduction modulo the maximal ideal of the local ring at
2022	  \(t\) -- the fibre map \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)
2023	  \cong \kappa(t)^{d}\) -- is surjective. A surjection of vector spaces \(\kappa(t)^{r}
2024	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa(t)^{d}\) carries some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard basis vectors to
2025	  a basis of \(\kappa(t)^{d}\); choosing such an index set \(I\) of size \(d\), the
2026	  corresponding \(d \times d\) minor \(M_I(t)\) is invertible over \(\kappa(t)\), i.e.\ its
2027	  determinant \(P_I = \det(M_I)\) does not vanish at \(t\). On the basic open
2028	  \(V_{P_I} = \{P_I \ne 0\} \subseteq V\), the section \(P_I\) is a unit, so the matrix
2029	  \(M_I\) is invertible and the chart composite \(s_I \circ q\) restricts to an isomorphism
2030	  there (this is the source mechanism: the locus where a minor determinant is invertible is
2031	  open, and the minor matrix admits an inverse on it). The implication ``minor invertible at
2032	  \(t\) \(\Rightarrow\) chart composite invertible on a neighbourhood'' is the Nakayama step:
2033	  invertibility of \((s_I \circ q)(t)\) on the fibre, together with finite generation, forces
2034	  invertibility on a neighbourhood. Hence \(t \in V_{P_I} \subseteq T_I\), and the \(T_I\)
2035	  cover \(T\).

2057	  adjunction a unique \emph{chart morphism} \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
2058	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\), and thus
2059	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q|_{T_I}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). The chart loci cover \(T\)
2060	  (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}), and on each overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two
2061	  presenting matrices are change-of-basis related by the invertible \(I\)-columns minor,
2062	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
2063	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}); hence the composites \(T_I \xrightarrow{\varphi_I} U^I
2064	  \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with the chart immersions agree on overlaps after the
2065	  transition \(\theta_{I,J}\). By the cocycle condition (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) these
2066	  chart-local morphisms glue to a single morphism \(T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
2067	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}); this glued morphism is
2068	  \(\mathrm{grPointOfRankQuotient}(x)\), the local-to-global inverse of the universal
2069	  property: pulling the tautological quotient back along it recovers \(\langle \mathcal{F},
2070	  q\rangle\) up to equivalence.
2071	\end{definition}
2072	
2073	\begin{theorem}
2074	
2075	\leanok
2076	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
2077	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
2078	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
2079	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
2080	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
2081	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
2082	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
2083	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
2084	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
2085	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2086	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
2087	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
2088	  %  contravariant functor
2089	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
2090	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
2091	  %  set of all equivalence classes
2092	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
2093	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
2094	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
2095	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
2096	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
2097	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
2098	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
2099	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
2100	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
2101	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
2102	  \[
2103	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
2104	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2105	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
2106	    \qquad
2107	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
2108	      \;\longmapsto\;
2109	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
2110	  \]
2111	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
2112	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
2113	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
2114	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
2115	  universal family.
2116	\end{theorem}
2117	
2118	\begin{proof}
2119	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
2120	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
2121	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
2122	    def:grassmannian_functor, lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi,
2123	    def:grPointOfRankQuotient, lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover, def:chartLocus,
2124	    lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
2125	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
2126	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
2127	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
2128	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
2129	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
2130	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
2131	
2132	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
2133	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
2134	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
2135	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
2136	  the composite
2137	  \[
2138	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
2139	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2140	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
2141	  \]
2142	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
2143	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2144	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
2145	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
2146	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
2147	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
2148	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
2149	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
2150	
2151	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
2152	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
2153	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
2154	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
2155	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
2156	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
2157	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
2158	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
2159	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
2160	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
2161	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
2162	
2163	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
2164	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
2165	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
2166	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
2167	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
2168	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
2169	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
2170	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
2171	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
2172	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
2173	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
2174	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
2175	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
2176	
2177	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
2178	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
2179	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
2180	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
2181	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
2182	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
2183	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
2184	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
2185	
2186	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
2187	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
2188	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
2189	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
2190	\end{proof}

This is a valid proof strategy.

The `% NOTE (review iter-078)` at lines 2006–2014 documents that the **formalized proof uses a different route** — affine-projective splitting:

2. `exists_isUnit_submatrix` — a right-invertible matrix over a field has an invertible d-column minor  
3. Cut out the basic open from the minor determinant

Both routes reach the same conclusion (minor invertible on a basic open implies `t ∈ T_I`), but the mechanism is different. A prover following the prose would attempt the Nakayama route and not match the existing Lean proof.

**Required action:** Blueprint-writer should rewrite the proof block to describe the affine-splitting route, per the existing NOTE. This is a documentation correctness issue, not a mathematical error. The active prover can consult the Lean source directly.
